\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{dropped =} \KeywordTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(temp_drop }\OperatorTok{>}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Drop"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"No Drop"}\NormalTok{))}

\NormalTok{sepsis }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{tabyl}\NormalTok{(treat, dropped) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{adorn_totals}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{adorn_percentages}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{denom =} \StringTok{"row"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{adorn_pct_formatting}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{digits =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{adorn_ns}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{position =} \StringTok{"front"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
     treat        Drop     No Drop
 Ibuprofen 107 (71.3%)  43 (28.7%)
   Placebo  80 (53.3%)  70 (46.7%)
     Total 187 (62.3%) 113 (37.7%)
\end{verbatim}

In our sample, we observe 71.3\% of the 150 Ibuprofen subjects with a positive \texttt{temp\_drop} as compared to 53.3\% of the 150 Placebo subjects. We want to compare these two probabilities (represented using proportions as 0.713 vs.~0.533) to estimate the size of the difference between the proportions with a point estimate and 90\% confidence interval.

But there are other comparisons we could make, too. The tricky part is that we have multiple ways to describe the relationship between treatment and outcome. We might compare outcome ``risks'' directly using the difference in probabilities, or the ratio of the two probabilities, or we might convert the risks to odds, and compare the ratio of those odds. In any case, we'll get different point estimates and confidence intervals, all of which will help us make conclusions about the evidence available in this trial speaking to differences between Ibuprofen and Placebo.

\hypertarget{relating-a-treatment-to-an-outcome}{%
\section{Relating a Treatment to an Outcome}\label{relating-a-treatment-to-an-outcome}}

The question of interest is whether the percentage of subjects whose temperature dropped is different (and probably larger) in the subjects who received Ibuprofen than in those who received the Placebo.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}rrrrr@{}}
\toprule
Treatment Arm & Dropped & Did Not Drop & Total & Proportion who dropped\tabularnewline
\midrule
\endhead
Ibuprofen & 107 & 43 & 150 & 0.713\tabularnewline
Placebo & 80 & 70 & 150 & 0.533\tabularnewline
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}

In other words, what is the relationship between the treatment and the outcome?

\hypertarget{definitions-of-probability-and-odds}{%
\section{Definitions of Probability and Odds}\label{definitions-of-probability-and-odds}}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Proportion = Probability = Risk of the trait = number with trait / total
\item
  Odds of having the trait = (number with the trait / number without the trait) to 1
\end{itemize}

If p is the proportion of subjects with a trait, then the \textbf{odds} of having the trait are \(\frac{p}{1-p}\) to 1.

So, the probability of a good result (temperature drop) in this case is \(\frac{107}{150} = 0.713\) in the Ibuprofen group. The \textbf{odds} of a good result are thus \(\frac{0.713}{1 - 0.713} = 2.484\) to 1 in the Ibuprofen group.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}rrrrrr@{}}
\toprule
Treatment Arm & Dropped & Did Not Drop & Total & Pr(dropped) & Odds(dropped)\tabularnewline
\midrule
\endhead
Ibuprofen & 107 & 43 & 150 & 0.713 & 2.484\tabularnewline
Placebo & 80 & 70 & 150 & 0.533 & 1.141\tabularnewline
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}

\hypertarget{defining-the-relative-risk}{%
\section{Defining the Relative Risk}\label{defining-the-relative-risk}}

Among the Ibuprofen subjects, the risk of a good outcome (drop in temperature) is 71.3\% or, stated as a proportion, 0.713. Among the Placebo subjects, the risk of a good outcome is 53.3\% or, stated as a proportion, 0.533.

Our ``crude'' estimate of the \textbf{relative risk} of a good outcome for Ibuprofen subjects as compared to Placebo subjects, is the ratio of these two risks, or 0.713/0.533 = 1.338

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The fact that this relative risk is greater than 1 indicates that the probability of a good outcome is higher for Ibuprofen subjects than for Placebo subjects.
\item
  A relative risk of 1 would indicate that the probability of a good outcome is the same for Ibuprofen subjects and for Placebo subjects.
\item
  A relative risk less than 1 would indicate that the probability of a good outcome is lower for Ibuprofen subjects than for Placebo subjects.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{defining-the-risk-difference}{%
\section{Defining the Risk Difference}\label{defining-the-risk-difference}}

Our ``crude'' estimate of the \textbf{risk difference} of a good outcome for Ibuprofen subjects as compared to Placebo subjects, is 0.713 - 0.533 = 0.180 or 18.0 percentage points.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The fact that this risk difference is greater than 0 indicates that the probability of a good outcome is higher for Ibuprofen subjects than for Placebo subjects.
\item
  A risk difference of 0 would indicate that the probability of a good outcome is the same for Ibuprofen subjects and for Placebo subjects.
\item
  A risk difference less than 0 would indicate that the probability of a good outcome is lower for Ibuprofen subjects than for Placebo subjects.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{defining-the-odds-ratio-or-the-cross-product-ratio}{%
\section{Defining the Odds Ratio, or the Cross-Product Ratio}\label{defining-the-odds-ratio-or-the-cross-product-ratio}}

Among the Ibuprofen subjects, the odds of a good outcome (temperature drop) are 2.484. Among the placebo subjects, the odds of a good outcome (temperature drop) are 1.141.

So our ``crude'' estimate of the \textbf{odds ratio} of a good outcome for Ibuprofen subjects as compared to placebo subjects, is 2.484 / 1.141 = 2.18

Another way to calculate this odds ratio is to calculate the \textbf{cross-product ratio}, which is equal to (a x d) / (b x c), for the 2 by 2 table with counts specified as shown:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}rrr@{}}
\toprule
A Generic Table & Good Outcome & Bad Outcome\tabularnewline
\midrule
\endhead
Treatment Group 1 & a & b\tabularnewline
Treatment Group 2 & c & d\tabularnewline
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}

So, for our table, we have a = 107, b = 43, c = 80, and d = 70, so the cross-product ratio is \(\frac{107 x 70}{43 x 80} = \frac{7490}{3440} = 2.18\). As expected, this is the same as the ``crude'' odds ratio estimate.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The fact that this odds ratio risk is greater than 1 indicates that the odds of a good outcome are higher for Ibuprofen subjects than for Placebo subjects.
\item
  An odds ratio of 1 would indicate that the odds of a good outcome are the same for Ibuprofen subjects and for Placebo subjects.
\item
  An odds ratio less than 1 would indicate that the odds of a good outcome are lower for Ibuprofen subjects than for Placebo subjects.
\end{itemize}

So, we have several different ways to compare the outcomes across the treatments. Are these differences and ratios large enough to rule out chance?

\hypertarget{comparing-rates-in-a-2x2-table}{%
\section{Comparing Rates in a 2x2 Table}\label{comparing-rates-in-a-2x2-table}}

What is the relationship between the treatment (Ibuprofen vs.~Placebo) and the outcome (drop in temperature) in the following two-by-two table?

\hypertarget{the-twobytwo-function-in-r}{%
\section{\texorpdfstring{The \texttt{twobytwo} function in R}{The twobytwo function in R}}\label{the-twobytwo-function-in-r}}

I built the \texttt{twobytwo} function in R (based on existing functions in the \texttt{Epi} library, which you need to have in your installed packages list in order for this to work) to do the work for this problem. All that is required is a single command, and a two-by-two table in standard epidemiological format (with the outcomes in the columns, and the treatments in the rows.)

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}rrr@{}}
\toprule
Treatment Arm & Dropped & Did Not Drop\tabularnewline
\midrule
\endhead
Ibuprofen & 107 & 43\tabularnewline
Placebo & 80 & 70\tabularnewline
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}

The command just requires you to read off the cells of the table, followed by the labels for the two treatments, then the two outcomes, then a specification of the names of the rows (exposures) and columns (outcomes) from the table, and a specification of the confidence level you desire. We'll use 90\% here.

The resulting output follows.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{twobytwo}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{107}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{43}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{80}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{70}\NormalTok{, }
         \StringTok{"Ibuprofen"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Placebo"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Dropped"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"No Drop"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{conf.level =} \FloatTok{0.90}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
2 by 2 table analysis: 
------------------------------------------------------ 
Outcome   : Dropped 
Comparing : Ibuprofen vs. Placebo 

          Dropped No Drop    P(Dropped) 90% conf. interval
Ibuprofen     107      43        0.7133    0.6490   0.7701
Placebo        80      70        0.5333    0.4661   0.5993

                                   90% conf. interval
             Relative Risk: 1.3375    1.1492   1.5567
         Sample Odds Ratio: 2.1773    1.4583   3.2509
Conditional MLE Odds Ratio: 2.1716    1.4177   3.3437
    Probability difference: 0.1800    0.0881   0.2677

             Exact P-value: 0.0019 
        Asymptotic P-value: 0.0014 
------------------------------------------------------
\end{verbatim}

\hypertarget{standard-epidemiological-format}{%
\subsection{Standard Epidemiological Format}\label{standard-epidemiological-format}}

This table is in \textbf{standard epidemiological format}, which means that:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The rows of the table describe the ``treatment'' (which we'll take here to be \texttt{treat}). The more interesting (sometimes also the more common) ``treatment'' is placed in the top row. That's Ibuprofen here.
\item
  The columns of the table describe the ``outcome'' (which we'll take here to be whether the subject's temperature dropped.) Typically, the more common ``outcome'' is placed to the left.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{outcome-probabilities-and-confidence-intervals-within-the-treatment-groups}{%
\subsection{Outcome Probabilities and Confidence Intervals Within the Treatment Groups}\label{outcome-probabilities-and-confidence-intervals-within-the-treatment-groups}}

The \texttt{twobytwo} output starts with estimates of the probability (risk) of a ``Dropped'' outcome among subjects who fall into the two treatment groups (Ibuprofen or Placebo), along with 90\% confidence intervals for each of these probabilities.

\begin{verbatim}
2 by 2 table analysis: 
------------------------------------------------------ 
Outcome   : Dropped 
Comparing : Ibuprofen vs. Placebo 

          Dropped No Drop    P(Dropped) 90% conf. interval
Ibuprofen     107      43        0.7133    0.6490   0.7701
Placebo        80      70        0.5333    0.4661   0.5993
\end{verbatim}

The conditional probability of a temperature drop given that the subject is in the Ibuprofen group, is symbolized as Pr(Dropped \textbar{} Ibuprofen) = 0.7133, and the 90\% confidence interval around that proportion is (0.6490, 0.7701).

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Note that these two confidence intervals fail to overlap, and so we expect to see a fairly large difference in the estimated probability of a temperature drop when we compare Ibuprofen to Placebo.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{relative-risk-odds-ratio-and-risk-difference-with-confidence-intervals}{%
\subsection{Relative Risk, Odds Ratio and Risk Difference, with Confidence Intervals}\label{relative-risk-odds-ratio-and-risk-difference-with-confidence-intervals}}

These elements are followed by estimates of the relative risk, odds ratio, and risk difference, each with associated 90\% confidence intervals.

\begin{verbatim}
                                   90% conf. interval
             Relative Risk: 1.3375    1.1492   1.5567
         Sample Odds Ratio: 2.1773    1.4583   3.2509
Conditional MLE Odds Ratio: 2.1716    1.4177   3.3437
    Probability difference: 0.1800    0.0881   0.2677
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The \textbf{relative risk}, or the ratio of P(Temperature Drop \textbar{} Ibuprofen) to P(Temperature Drop \textbar{} Placebo), is shown first. Note that the 90\% confidence interval is entirely greater than 1.
\item
  The \textbf{odds ratio} is presented using two different definitions (the sample odds ratio is the cross-product ratio we mentioned earlier). Note that the 90\% confidence interval using either approach is entirely greater than 1.
\item
  The \textbf{probability} (or risk) \textbf{difference} {[}P(Temperature Drop \textbar{} Ibuprofen) - P(Temperature Drop \textbar{} Placebo){]} is presented last. Note that the 90\% confidence interval is entirely greater than 0.
\item
  Note carefully that if there had been no difference between Ibuprofen and Placebo, the relative risk and odds ratios would be 1, but the probability difference would be zero.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{estimating-a-rate-more-accurately-use-x-2n-4-rather-than-xn}{%
\section{Estimating a Rate More Accurately: Use (x + 2)/(n + 4) rather than x/n}\label{estimating-a-rate-more-accurately-use-x-2n-4-rather-than-xn}}

Suppose you have some data involving n independent tries, with x successes. A natural estimate of the ``success rate'' in the data is x / n.~

But, strangely enough, it turns out this isn't an entirely satisfying estimator. Alan Agresti provides substantial motivation for the (x + 2)/(n + 4) estimate as an alternative\footnote{This note comes largely from a May 15 2007 entry in Andrew Gelman's blog at http://andrewgelman.com/2007/05/15}. This is sometimes called a \emph{Bayesian augmentation}.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The big problem with x / n is that it estimates p = 0 or p = 1 when x = 0 or x = n.~
\item
  It's also tricky to compute confidence intervals at these extremes, since the usual standard error for a proportion, \(\sqrt{n p (1-p)}\), gives zero, which isn't quite right.
\item
  (x + 2)/(n + 4) is much cleaner, especially when you build a confidence interval for the rate.
\item
  The only place where (x + 2)/(n + 4) will go wrong is if n is small and the true probability is very close to 0 or 1.
\end{itemize}

For example, if n = 10, and p is 1 in a million, then x will almost certainly be zero, and an estimate of 1/12 is much worse than the simple 0/10. However, how big a deal is this? If p might be 1 in a million, you're not going to estimate it with a n = 10 experiment\footnote{Andrew Gelman's example is "I'm not going to try ten 100-foot golf putts, miss all of them, and then estimate my probability of success as 1/12."}.

Applying this method to our Ibuprofen and Sepsis Trial data, we would simply add two to each frequency in the main four cells in our 2x2 table.

So instead of using

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{twobytwo}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{107}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{43}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{80}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{70}\NormalTok{, }
         \StringTok{"Ibuprofen"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Placebo"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Dropped"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"No Drop"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{conf.level =} \FloatTok{0.90}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

the Bayesian augmentation would encourage us to look at

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{twobytwo}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{109}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{45}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{82}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{72}\NormalTok{, }
         \StringTok{"Ibuprofen"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Placebo"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Dropped"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"No Drop"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{conf.level =} \FloatTok{0.90}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
2 by 2 table analysis: 
------------------------------------------------------ 
Outcome   : Dropped 
Comparing : Ibuprofen vs. Placebo 

          Dropped No Drop    P(Dropped) 90% conf. interval
Ibuprofen     109      45        0.7078    0.6441   0.7643
Placebo        82      72        0.5325    0.4662   0.5977

                                   90% conf. interval
             Relative Risk: 1.3293    1.1434   1.5453
         Sample Odds Ratio: 2.1268    1.4337   3.1550
Conditional MLE Odds Ratio: 2.1215    1.3950   3.2421
    Probability difference: 0.1753    0.0845   0.2622

             Exact P-value: 0.0022 
        Asymptotic P-value: 0.0016 
------------------------------------------------------
\end{verbatim}

As you can see, the odds ratio and relative risk estimates are (a little) closer to 1, and the probability difference is also a little closer to 0. The Bayesian augmentation provides a slightly more conservative set of estimates of the impact of Ibuprofen as compared to Placebo.

It is likely that the augmented version is a more accurate estimate here, but the two estimates will be comparable, generally, so long as either (a) the sample size in each exposure group is more than, say, 30 subjects, and/or (b) the sample probability of the outcome is between 10\% and 90\% in each exposure group.

\hypertarget{a-second-example-ebola-virus-disease-study-again}{%
\section{A Second Example: Ebola Virus Disease Study, again}\label{a-second-example-ebola-virus-disease-study-again}}

For instance, recall the Ebola Virus Disease study from the \emph{New England Journal of Medicine} that we described in the previous Chapter. Suppose we want to compare the proportion of deaths among cases that had a definitive outcome who were hospitalized to the proportion of deaths among cases that had a definitive outcome who were not hospitalized.

The article suggests that of the 1,737 cases with a definitive outcome, there were 1,153 hospitalized cases. Across those 1,153 hospitalized cases, 741 people (64.3\%) died, which means that across the remaining 584 non-hospitalized cases, 488 people (83.6\%) died.

Here is the initial contingency table, using only the numbers from the previous paragraph.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}rrrr@{}}
\toprule
Initial Ebola Table & Deceased & Alive & Total\tabularnewline
\midrule
\endhead
Hospitalized & 741 & -- & 1153\tabularnewline
Not Hospitalized & 488 & -- & 584\tabularnewline
Total & & & 1737\tabularnewline
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}

Now, we can use arithmetic to complete the table, since the rows and the columns are each mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}rrrr@{}}
\toprule
Ebola 2x2 Table & Deceased & Alive & Total\tabularnewline
\midrule
\endhead
Hospitalized & 741 & 412 & 1153\tabularnewline
Not Hospitalized & 488 & 96 & 584\tabularnewline
Total & 1229 & 508 & 1737\tabularnewline
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}

We want to compare the fatality risk (probability of being in the deceased column) for the population of people in the hospitalized row to the population of people in the not hospitalized row.

We can run these data through R, using the Bayesian augmentation (adding a death and a survival to the hospitalized and also to the not hospitalized groups.) We'll use a 95\% confidence level this time.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{twobytwo}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{741}\OperatorTok{+}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{412}\OperatorTok{+}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{488}\OperatorTok{+}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{96}\OperatorTok{+}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }
         \StringTok{"Hospitalized"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Not Hospitalized"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Deceased"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Alive"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{conf.level =} \FloatTok{0.95}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
2 by 2 table analysis: 
------------------------------------------------------ 
Outcome   : Deceased 
Comparing : Hospitalized vs. Not Hospitalized 

                 Deceased Alive    P(Deceased) 95% conf. interval
Hospitalized          743   414         0.6422    0.6141   0.6693
Not Hospitalized      490    98         0.8333    0.8010   0.8613

                                    95% conf. interval
             Relative Risk:  0.7706    0.7285   0.8151
         Sample Odds Ratio:  0.3589    0.2801   0.4599
Conditional MLE Odds Ratio:  0.3591    0.2772   0.4624
    Probability difference: -0.1912   -0.2307  -0.1490

             Exact P-value: 0.0000 
        Asymptotic P-value: 0.0000 
------------------------------------------------------
\end{verbatim}

I'll leave it as an exercise for you to interpret these results and draw some conclusions.

\hypertarget{larger-contingency-tables}{%
\chapter{Larger Contingency Tables}\label{larger-contingency-tables}}

What will we do with tables describing data from more than two categories at a time, returning to the notion of independent (rather than paired or matched) samples? The chi-square tests we have already seen in our \texttt{twobytwo} table output will extend nicely to this scenario, especially the Pearson \(\chi^2\) (asymptotic) test.

\hypertarget{a-2x3-table-comparing-response-to-active-vs.-placebo}{%
\section{A 2x3 Table: Comparing Response to Active vs.~Placebo}\label{a-2x3-table-comparing-response-to-active-vs.-placebo}}

The table below, containing 2 rows and 3 columns of data (ignoring the marginal totals) specifies the number of patients who show \emph{complete}, \emph{partial}, or \emph{no response} after treatment with either \textbf{active} medication or a \textbf{placebo}.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}rccc@{}}
\toprule
Group & None & Partial & Complete\tabularnewline
\midrule
\endhead
Active & 16 & 26 & 29\tabularnewline
Placebo & 24 & 26 & 18\tabularnewline
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}

Is there a statistically significant association here? That is to say, is there a statistically significant difference between the treatment groups in the distribution of responses?

\hypertarget{getting-the-table-into-r}{%
\subsection{Getting the Table into R}\label{getting-the-table-into-r}}

To answer this, we'll have to get the data from this contingency table into a matrix in R. Here's one approach\ldots{}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{T1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{16}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{26}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{29}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{24}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{26}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{18}\NormalTok{), }\DataTypeTok{ncol=}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{nrow=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{byrow=}\OtherTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{rownames}\NormalTok{(T1) <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Active"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Placebo"}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{colnames}\NormalTok{(T1) <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"None"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Partial"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Complete"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{T1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
        None Partial Complete
Active    16      26       29
Placebo   24      26       18
\end{verbatim}

\hypertarget{manipulating-the-tables-presentation}{%
\subsection{Manipulating the Table's presentation}\label{manipulating-the-tables-presentation}}

We can add margins to the matrix to get a table including row and column totals.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{addmargins}\NormalTok{(T1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
        None Partial Complete Sum
Active    16      26       29  71
Placebo   24      26       18  68
Sum       40      52       47 139
\end{verbatim}

Instead of the counts, we can tabulate the proportion of all patients within each cell.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{prop.table}\NormalTok{(T1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
             None   Partial  Complete
Active  0.1151079 0.1870504 0.2086331
Placebo 0.1726619 0.1870504 0.1294964
\end{verbatim}

Now, to actually obtain a \emph{p} value and perform the significance test with \(H_0\): rows and columns are independent vs.~\(H_A\): rows and columns are associated, we simply run a Pearson chi-square test on T1 \ldots{}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{chisq.test}\NormalTok{(T1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

	Pearson's Chi-squared test

data:  T1
X-squared = 4.1116, df = 2, p-value = 0.128
\end{verbatim}

Thanks to a p-value of about 0.13 (using the Pearson chi-square test) our conclusion would be to retain the null hypothesis of independence in this setting.

We could have run a Fisher's exact test, too, if we needed it.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{fisher.test}\NormalTok{(T1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

	Fisher's Exact Test for Count Data

data:  T1
p-value = 0.1346
alternative hypothesis: two.sided
\end{verbatim}

The Fisher exact test \emph{p} value is also 0.13. Either way, there is insufficient evidence to conclude that there is a (true) difference in the distributions of responses.

\hypertarget{accuracy-of-death-certificates-a-6x3-table}{%
\section{Accuracy of Death Certificates (A 6x3 Table)}\label{accuracy-of-death-certificates-a-6x3-table}}

The table below compiles data from six studies designed to investigate the accuracy of death certificates. The original citation is Kircher T, Nelson J, Burdo H (1985) The autopsy as a measure of accuracy of the death certificate. \emph{NEJM}, 313, 1263-1269. 5373 autopsies were compared to the causes of death listed on the certificates. Of those, 3726 were confirmed to be accurate, 783 either lacked information or contained inaccuracies but did not require recoding of the underlying cause of death, and 864 were incorrect and required recoding. Do the results across studies appear consistent?

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}ccccc@{}}
\toprule
\begin{minipage}[b]{0.14\columnwidth}\centering
Date of Study\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
{[}Confirmed{]} Accurate\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{0.24\columnwidth}\centering
{[}Inaccurate{]} No Change\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
{[}Incorrect{]} Recoding\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{0.05\columnwidth}\centering
Total\strut
\end{minipage}\tabularnewline
\midrule
\endhead
\begin{minipage}[t]{0.14\columnwidth}\centering
1955-1965\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
2040\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.24\columnwidth}\centering
367\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
327\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.05\columnwidth}\centering
2734\strut
\end{minipage}\tabularnewline
\begin{minipage}[t]{0.14\columnwidth}\centering
1970\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
149\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.24\columnwidth}\centering
60\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
48\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.05\columnwidth}\centering
257\strut
\end{minipage}\tabularnewline
\begin{minipage}[t]{0.14\columnwidth}\centering
1970-1971\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
288\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.24\columnwidth}\centering
25\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
70\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.05\columnwidth}\centering
383\strut
\end{minipage}\tabularnewline
\begin{minipage}[t]{0.14\columnwidth}\centering
1975-1977\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
703\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.24\columnwidth}\centering
197\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
252\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.05\columnwidth}\centering
1152\strut
\end{minipage}\tabularnewline
\begin{minipage}[t]{0.14\columnwidth}\centering
1977-1978\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
425\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.24\columnwidth}\centering
62\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
88\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.05\columnwidth}\centering
575\strut
\end{minipage}\tabularnewline
\begin{minipage}[t]{0.14\columnwidth}\centering
1980\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
121\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.24\columnwidth}\centering
72\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
79\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.05\columnwidth}\centering
272\strut
\end{minipage}\tabularnewline
\begin{minipage}[t]{0.14\columnwidth}\centering
Total\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
3726\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.24\columnwidth}\centering
783\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.21\columnwidth}\centering
864\strut
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[t]{0.05\columnwidth}\centering
5373\strut
\end{minipage}\tabularnewline
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}

\hypertarget{the-pearson-chi-square-test-of-independence}{%
\section{The Pearson Chi-Square Test of Independence}\label{the-pearson-chi-square-test-of-independence}}

We can assess the homogeneity of the confirmation results (columns) we observe in the table using a Pearson chi-squared test of independence.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The null hypothesis is that the rows and columns are independent.
\item
  The alternative hypothesis is that there is an association between the rows and the columns.
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{death.tab <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2040}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{367}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{327}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{149}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{48}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{288}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{25}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{70}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{703}\NormalTok{,}
                      \DecValTok{197}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{252}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{425}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{62}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{88}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{121}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{72}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{79}\NormalTok{), }\DataTypeTok{byrow=}\OtherTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{nrow=}\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{rownames}\NormalTok{(death.tab) <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"1955-65"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"1970"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"1970-71"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"1975-77"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"1977-78"}\NormalTok{,}
                 \StringTok{"1980"}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{colnames}\NormalTok{(death.tab) <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Confirmed"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Inaccurate"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Incorrect"}\NormalTok{)}
 
\KeywordTok{addmargins}\NormalTok{(death.tab)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
        Confirmed Inaccurate Incorrect  Sum
1955-65      2040        367       327 2734
1970          149         60        48  257
1970-71       288         25        70  383
1975-77       703        197       252 1152
1977-78       425         62        88  575
1980          121         72        79  272
Sum          3726        783       864 5373
\end{verbatim}

To see the potential heterogeneity across rows in these data, we should perhaps also look at the proportions of autopsies in each of the three accuracy categories for each study.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{addmargins}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{100}\OperatorTok{*}\KeywordTok{prop.table}\NormalTok{(death.tab,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
        Confirmed Inaccurate Incorrect Sum
1955-65      74.6       13.4      12.0 100
1970         58.0       23.3      18.7 100
1970-71      75.2        6.5      18.3 100
1975-77      61.0       17.1      21.9 100
1977-78      73.9       10.8      15.3 100
1980         44.5       26.5      29.0 100
\end{verbatim}

In three of the studies, approximately 3/4 of the results were confirmed. In the other three, 45\%, 58\% and 61\% were confirmed. It looks like there's a fair amount of variation in results across studies. To see if this is true, formally, we run Pearson's chi-square test of independence, where the null hypothesis is that the rows and columns are independent, and the alternative hypothesis is that there is an association between the rows and the columns.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{chisq.test}\NormalTok{(death.tab)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

	Pearson's Chi-squared test

data:  death.tab
X-squared = 209.09, df = 10, p-value < 2.2e-16
\end{verbatim}

The chi-square test statistic is 200 on 10 degrees of freedom, yielding p \textless{} 0.0001.

Autopsies are not performed at random; in fact, many are done because the cause of death listed on the certificate is uncertain. What problems may arise if you attempt to use the results of these studies to make inference about the population as a whole?

\hypertarget{three-way-tables-a-2x2xk-table-and-a-mantel-haenszel-analysis}{%
\section{Three-Way Tables: A 2x2xK Table and a Mantel-Haenszel Analysis}\label{three-way-tables-a-2x2xk-table-and-a-mantel-haenszel-analysis}}

The material I discuss in this section is attributable to Jeff Simonoff and his book \emph{Analyzing Categorical Data}. The example is taken from Section 8.1 of that book.

A three-dimensional or three-way table of counts often reflects a situation where the rows and columns refer to variables whose association is of primary interest to us, and the third factor (a layer, or strata) describes a control variable, whose effect on our primary association is something we are \emph{controlling} for in the analysis.

\hypertarget{smoking-and-mortality-in-the-uk}{%
\subsection{Smoking and Mortality in the UK}\label{smoking-and-mortality-in-the-uk}}

In the early 1970s and then again 20 years later, in Whickham, United Kingdom, surveys yielded the following relationship between whether a person was a smoker at the time of the original survey and whether they were still alive 20 years later\footnote{See Appleton et al. 1996. Ignoring a Covariate: An Example of Simpson's Paradox. The American Statistician, 50, 340-341.}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{whickham1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{502}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{230}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{443}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{139}\NormalTok{), }\DataTypeTok{byrow=}\OtherTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{nrow=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{rownames}\NormalTok{(whickham1) <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Non-Smoker"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Smoker"}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{colnames}\NormalTok{(whickham1) <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Alive"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Dead"}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{addmargins}\NormalTok{(whickham1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
           Alive Dead  Sum
Non-Smoker   502  230  732
Smoker       443  139  582
Sum          945  369 1314
\end{verbatim}

Here's the two-by-two table analysis.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{Epi}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{twoby2}\NormalTok{(whickham1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
2 by 2 table analysis: 
------------------------------------------------------ 
Outcome   : Alive 
Comparing : Non-Smoker vs. Smoker 

           Alive Dead    P(Alive) 95% conf. interval
Non-Smoker   502  230      0.6858    0.6512   0.7184
Smoker       443  139      0.7612    0.7248   0.7941

                                    95% conf. interval
             Relative Risk:  0.9010    0.8427   0.9633
         Sample Odds Ratio:  0.6848    0.5353   0.8761
Conditional MLE Odds Ratio:  0.6850    0.5307   0.8822
    Probability difference: -0.0754   -0.1230  -0.0266

             Exact P-value: 0.0030 
        Asymptotic P-value: 0.0026 
------------------------------------------------------
\end{verbatim}

There is a detectable association between smoking and mortality, but it isn't the one you might expect.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The odds ratio is 0.68, implying that the odds of having lived were only 68\% as large for non-smokers as for smokers.
\item
  Does that mean that smoking is \emph{good} for you?
\end{itemize}

Not likely. There is a key ``lurking'' variable here - a variable that is related to both smoking and mortality that is obscuring the actual relationship - namely, age.

\hypertarget{the-whickham-data-with-age-too}{%
\subsection{\texorpdfstring{The \texttt{whickham} data with age, too}{The whickham data with age, too}}\label{the-whickham-data-with-age-too}}

The table below gives the mortality experience separated into subtables by initial age group.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{age <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"18-24"}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{), }\KeywordTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"25-34"}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{), }
         \KeywordTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"35-44"}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{), }\KeywordTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"45-54"}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{), }
         \KeywordTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"55-64"}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{), }\KeywordTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"65-74"}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{), }
         \KeywordTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"75+"}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{smoking <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Smoker"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Smoker"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Non-Smoker"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Non-Smoker"}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{7}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{status <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Alive"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Dead"}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{14}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{counts <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{53}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{61}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{121}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{152}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{,}
            \DecValTok{95}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{14}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{114}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{7}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{103}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{27}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{66}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{,}
            \DecValTok{64}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{51}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{81}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{40}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{7}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{29}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{28}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{101}\NormalTok{,}
            \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{13}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{64}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{whickham2 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(smoking, status, age, counts) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{smoking =} \KeywordTok{factor}\NormalTok{(smoking),}
           \DataTypeTok{status =} \KeywordTok{factor}\NormalTok{(status),}
           \DataTypeTok{age =} \KeywordTok{factor}\NormalTok{(age))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{whickham2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 28 x 4
   smoking    status age   counts
   <fct>      <fct>  <fct>  <dbl>
 1 Smoker     Alive  18-24     53
 2 Smoker     Dead   18-24      2
 3 Non-Smoker Alive  18-24     61
 4 Non-Smoker Dead   18-24      1
 5 Smoker     Alive  25-34    121
 6 Smoker     Dead   25-34      3
 7 Non-Smoker Alive  25-34    152
 8 Non-Smoker Dead   25-34      5
 9 Smoker     Alive  35-44     95
10 Smoker     Dead   35-44     14
# ... with 18 more rows
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{whick_t2 <-}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{xtabs}\NormalTok{(counts }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{smoking }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{status }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{age, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ whickham2)}

\NormalTok{whick_t2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
, , age = 18-24

            status
smoking      Alive Dead
  Non-Smoker    61    1
  Smoker        53    2

, , age = 25-34

            status
smoking      Alive Dead
  Non-Smoker   152    5
  Smoker       121    3

, , age = 35-44

            status
smoking      Alive Dead
  Non-Smoker   114    7
  Smoker        95   14

, , age = 45-54

            status
smoking      Alive Dead
  Non-Smoker    66   12
  Smoker       103   27

, , age = 55-64

            status
smoking      Alive Dead
  Non-Smoker    81   40
  Smoker        64   51

, , age = 65-74

            status
smoking      Alive Dead
  Non-Smoker    28  101
  Smoker         7   29

, , age = 75+

            status
smoking      Alive Dead
  Non-Smoker     0   64
  Smoker         0   13
\end{verbatim}

The sample odds ratios for remaining Alive comparing Non-Smokers to Smokers for each of these subtables (except the last one, where the odds ratio is undefined because of the zero cells) are calculated using the usual cross-product ratio approach, which yields the following results.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}rr@{}}
\toprule
Age Group & Odds Ratio\tabularnewline
\midrule
\endhead
18-24 & 2.30\tabularnewline
25-34 & 0.75\tabularnewline
35-44 & 2.40\tabularnewline
45-54 & 1.44\tabularnewline
55-64 & 1.61\tabularnewline
65-74 & 1.15\tabularnewline
75+ & Undefined\tabularnewline
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}

Thus, for all age groups except 25-34 year olds, not smoking appears to be associated with higher odds of remaining alive.

Why? Not surprisingly, there is a strong association between age and mortality, with mortality rates being very low for young people (2.5\% for 18-24 year olds) and increasing to 100\% for 75+ year olds.

There is also an association between age and smoking, with smoking rates peaking in the 45-54 year old range and then falling off rapidly. In particular, respondents who were 65 and older at the time of the first survey had very low smoking rates (25.4\%) but very high mortality rates (85.5\%). Smoking was hardly the cause, however, since even among the 65-74 year olds mortality was higher among smokers (80.6\%) than it was among non-smokers (78.3\%). A flat version of the table (\texttt{ftable} in R) can help us with these calculations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{ftable}\NormalTok{(whick_t2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
                  age 18-24 25-34 35-44 45-54 55-64 65-74 75+
smoking    status                                            
Non-Smoker Alive         61   152   114    66    81    28   0
           Dead           1     5     7    12    40   101  64
Smoker     Alive         53   121    95   103    64     7   0
           Dead           2     3    14    27    51    29  13
\end{verbatim}

\hypertarget{checking-assumptions-the-woolf-test}{%
\subsection{Checking Assumptions: The Woolf test}\label{checking-assumptions-the-woolf-test}}

We can also obtain a test (using the \texttt{woolf\_test} function, in the \texttt{vcd} library) to see if the common odds ratio estimated in the Mantel-Haenszel procedure is reasonable for all age groups. In other words, the Woolf test is a test of the assumption of homogeneous odds ratios across the six age groups.

If the Woolf test is significant, it suggests that the Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel test is not appropriate, since the odds ratios for smoking and mortality vary too much in the sub-tables by age group. Here, we have the following log odds ratios (estimated using conditional maximum likelihood, rather than cross-product ratios) and the associated Woolf test.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{## Next two results use the vcd package}
\NormalTok{vcd}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{oddsratio}\NormalTok{(whick_t2, }\DataTypeTok{log =} \OtherTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
log odds ratios for smoking and status by age 

      18-24       25-34       35-44       45-54       55-64       65-74 
 0.65018114 -0.22473479  0.84069420  0.34608770  0.47421763  0.09933253 
        75+ 
-1.56397554 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{vcd}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{woolf_test}\NormalTok{(whick_t2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

	Woolf-test on Homogeneity of Odds Ratios (no 3-Way assoc.)

data:  whick_t2
X-squared = 3.2061, df = 6, p-value = 0.7826
\end{verbatim}

As you can see, the Woolf test is not close to our usual standards for statistically detectable results, implying the common odds ratio is at least potentially reasonable for all age groups (or at least the ones under ages 75, where some data are available.)

\hypertarget{the-cochran-mantel-haenszel-test}{%
\subsection{The Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel Test}\label{the-cochran-mantel-haenszel-test}}

So, the marginal table looking at smoking and mortality combining all age groups isn't the most meaningful summary of the relationship between smoking and mortality. Instead, we need to look at the \emph{conditional} association of smoking and mortality, \textbf{given age}, to address our interests.

The null hypothesis would be that, in the population, smoking and mortality are independent within strata formed by age group. In other words, \(H_0\) requires that smoking be of no value in predicting mortality once age has been accounted for.

The alternative hypothesis would be that, in the population, smoking and mortality are associated within the strata formed by age group. In other words, \(H_A\) requires that smoking be of at least some value in predicting mortality even after age has been accounted for.

We can consider the evidence that helps us choose between these two hypotheses with a Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel test, which is obtained in R through the \texttt{mantelhaen.test} function. This test requires us to assume that, in the population and within each age group, the smoking-mortality odds ratio is the same. Essentially, this means that the association of smoking with mortality is the same for older and younger people.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{mantelhaen.test}\NormalTok{(whick_t2, }\DataTypeTok{conf.level  =} \FloatTok{0.90}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

	Mantel-Haenszel chi-squared test with continuity correction

data:  whick_t2
Mantel-Haenszel X-squared = 5.435, df = 1, p-value = 0.01974
alternative hypothesis: true common odds ratio is not equal to 1
90 percent confidence interval:
 1.143198 2.041872
sample estimates:
common odds ratio 
          1.52783 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel test statistic is a bit larger than 5 (after a continuity correction) leading to a \emph{p} value of 0.02, indicating strong rejection of the null hypothesis of conditional independence of smoking and survival given age.
\item
  The estimated common conditional odds ratio is 1.53. This implies that (adjusting for age) being a non-smoker is associated with 53\% higher odds of being alive 20 years later than being a smoker.
\item
  A 90\% confidence interval for that common odds ratio is (1.14, 2.04), reinforcing rejection of the null hypothesis of conditional independence (where the odds ratio would be 1).
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{without-the-continuity-correction}{%
\subsection{Without the Continuity Correction}\label{without-the-continuity-correction}}

By default, R presents the Mantel-Haenszel test with a continuity correction, when used for a 2x2xK table. In virtually all cases, go ahead and do this, but as you can see below, the difference it makes in this case is modest.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{mantelhaen.test}\NormalTok{(whick_t2, }\DataTypeTok{correct=}\OtherTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{conf.level =} \FloatTok{0.90}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

	Mantel-Haenszel chi-squared test without continuity correction

data:  whick_t2
Mantel-Haenszel X-squared = 5.8443, df = 1, p-value = 0.01563
alternative hypothesis: true common odds ratio is not equal to 1
90 percent confidence interval:
 1.143198 2.041872
sample estimates:
common odds ratio 
          1.52783 
\end{verbatim}

\hypertarget{power-and-proportions}{%
\chapter{Power and Proportions}\label{power-and-proportions}}

\hypertarget{tuberculosis-prevalence-among-iv-drug-users}{%
\section{Tuberculosis Prevalence Among IV Drug Users}\label{tuberculosis-prevalence-among-iv-drug-users}}

Consider a study to investigate factors affecting tuberculosis prevalence among intravenous drug users. The original data source is Graham NMH et al.~(1992) Prevalence of Tuberculin Positivity and Skin Test Anergy in HIV-1-Seropositive and Seronegative Intravenous Drug Users. \emph{JAMA}, 267, 369-373. Among 97 individuals who admit to sharing needles, 24 (24.7\%) had a positive tuberculin skin test result; among 161 drug users who deny sharing needles, 28 (17.4\%) had a positive test result. To start, we'll test the null hypothesis that the proportions of intravenous drug users who have a positive tuberculin skin test result are identical for those who share needles and those who do not.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{twobytwo}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{24}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{73}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{28}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{133}\NormalTok{, }
         \StringTok{"Sharing Needles"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Not Sharing"}\NormalTok{, }
         \StringTok{"TB test+"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"TB test-"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
2 by 2 table analysis: 
------------------------------------------------------ 
Outcome   : TB test+ 
Comparing : Sharing Needles vs. Not Sharing 

                TB test+ TB test-    P(TB test+) 95% conf. interval
Sharing Needles       24       73         0.2474    0.1717   0.3427
Not Sharing           28      133         0.1739    0.1229   0.2404

                                   95% conf. interval
             Relative Risk: 1.4227    0.8772   2.3073
         Sample Odds Ratio: 1.5616    0.8439   2.8898
Conditional MLE Odds Ratio: 1.5588    0.8014   3.0191
    Probability difference: 0.0735   -0.0265   0.1807

             Exact P-value: 0.1996 
        Asymptotic P-value: 0.1557 
------------------------------------------------------
\end{verbatim}

What conclusions should we draw?

\hypertarget{designing-a-new-tb-study}{%
\section{Designing a New TB Study}\label{designing-a-new-tb-study}}

Now, suppose we wanted to design a new study with as many non-sharers as needle-sharers participating, and suppose that we wanted to detect any difference in the proportion of positive skin test results between the two groups that was identical to the data presented above or larger with at least 90\% power, using a two-sided test and \(\alpha\) = .05. What sample size would be required to accomplish these aims?

\hypertarget{using-power.prop.test-for-balanced-designs}{%
\section{\texorpdfstring{Using \texttt{power.prop.test} for Balanced Designs}{Using power.prop.test for Balanced Designs}}\label{using-power.prop.test-for-balanced-designs}}

Our constraints are that we want to find the sample size for a two-sample comparison of proportions using a balanced design, we will use \(\alpha\) = .05, and power = .90, and that we estimate that the non-sharers will have a .174 proportion of positive tests, and we will try to detect a difference between this group and the needle sharers, who we estimate will have a proportion of .247, using a two-sided hypothesis test.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{power.prop.test}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{p1 =} \FloatTok{.174}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{p2  =} \FloatTok{.247}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sig.level =} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{power =} \FloatTok{0.90}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

     Two-sample comparison of proportions power calculation 

              n = 653.2876
             p1 = 0.174
             p2 = 0.247
      sig.level = 0.05
          power = 0.9
    alternative = two.sided

NOTE: n is number in *each* group
\end{verbatim}

So, we'd need at least 654 non-sharing subjects, and 654 more who share needles to accomplish the aims of the study.

\hypertarget{how-power.prop.test-works}{%
\section{\texorpdfstring{How \texttt{power.prop.test} works}{How power.prop.test works}}\label{how-power.prop.test-works}}

\texttt{power.prop.test} works much like the \texttt{power.t.test} we saw for means.

Again, we specify 4 of the following 5 elements of the comparison, and R calculates the fifth.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The sample size (interpreted as the \# in each group, so half the total sample size)
\item
  The true probability in group 1
\item
  The true probability in group 2
\item
  The significance level (\(\alpha\))
\item
  The power (1 - \(\beta\))
\end{itemize}

The big weakness with the \texttt{power.prop.test} tool is that it doesn't allow you to work with unbalanced designs.

\hypertarget{a-revised-scenario}{%
\section{A Revised Scenario}\label{a-revised-scenario}}

Suppose we can get exactly 800 subjects in total (400 sharing and 400 non-sharing). How much power would we have to detect a difference in the proportion of positive skin test results between the two groups that was identical to the data presented above or larger, using a one-sided test, with \(\alpha\) = .10?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{power.prop.test}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{n=}\DecValTok{400}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{p1=}\NormalTok{.}\DecValTok{174}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{p2=}\NormalTok{.}\DecValTok{247}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sig.level =} \FloatTok{0.10}\NormalTok{,}
                \DataTypeTok{alternative=}\StringTok{"one.sided"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

     Two-sample comparison of proportions power calculation 

              n = 400
             p1 = 0.174
             p2 = 0.247
      sig.level = 0.1
          power = 0.8954262
    alternative = one.sided

NOTE: n is number in *each* group
\end{verbatim}

We would have just under 90\% power to detect such an effect.

\hypertarget{using-the-pwr-library-for-unbalanced-designs}{%
\section{\texorpdfstring{Using the \texttt{pwr} library for Unbalanced Designs}{Using the pwr library for Unbalanced Designs}}\label{using-the-pwr-library-for-unbalanced-designs}}

The \texttt{pwr.2p2n.test} function in the \texttt{pwr} library can help assess the power of a test to determine a particular effect size using an unbalanced design, where \(n_1\) is not equal to \(n_2\).

As before, we specify four of the following five elements of the comparison, and R calculates the fifth.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \texttt{n1} = The sample size in group 1
\item
  \texttt{n2} = The sample size in group 2
\item
  \texttt{sig.level} = The significance level (\(\alpha\))
\item
  \texttt{power} = The power (1 - \(\beta\))
\item
  \texttt{h} = the effect size h, which can be calculated separately in R based on the two proportions being compared: \texttt{p1} and \texttt{p2}.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{calculating-the-effect-size-h}{%
\subsection{\texorpdfstring{Calculating the Effect Size \texttt{h}}{Calculating the Effect Size h}}\label{calculating-the-effect-size-h}}

To calculate the effect size for a given set of proportions, just use \texttt{ES.h(p1,\ p2)} which is available in the \texttt{pwr} library.

For instance, in our comparison, we have the following effect size.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{pwr}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{ES.h}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{p1 =} \FloatTok{.174}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{p2 =} \FloatTok{.247}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] -0.1796783
\end{verbatim}

\hypertarget{using-pwr.2p2n.test-in-r}{%
\section{\texorpdfstring{Using \texttt{pwr.2p2n.test} in R}{Using pwr.2p2n.test in R}}\label{using-pwr.2p2n.test-in-r}}

Suppose we can have 700 samples in group 1 (the not sharing group) but only half that many in group 2 (the group of users who share needles). How much power would we have to detect this same difference (\(p_1\) = .174, \(p_2\) = .247) with a 5\% significance level in a two-sided test?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{pwr}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{pwr.2p2n.test}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{h =}\NormalTok{ pwr}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{ES.h}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{p1 =} \FloatTok{.174}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{p2 =} \FloatTok{.247}\NormalTok{), }
              \DataTypeTok{n1 =} \DecValTok{700}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{n2 =} \DecValTok{350}\NormalTok{,}
              \DataTypeTok{sig.level =} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

     difference of proportion power calculation for binomial distribution (arcsine transformation) 

              h = 0.1796783
             n1 = 700
             n2 = 350
      sig.level = 0.05
          power = 0.7836768
    alternative = two.sided

NOTE: different sample sizes
\end{verbatim}

Note that the headline for this output actually reads:

\begin{verbatim}
difference of proportion power calculation for binomial distribution 
(arcsine transformation) 
\end{verbatim}

It appears we will have about 78\% power under these circumstances.

\hypertarget{comparison-to-balanced-design}{%
\subsection{Comparison to Balanced Design}\label{comparison-to-balanced-design}}

How does this compare to the results with a balanced design using only 1000 drug users in total, so that we have 500 patients in each group?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{pwr}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{pwr.2p2n.test}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{h =}\NormalTok{ pwr}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{ES.h}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{p1 =} \FloatTok{.174}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{p2 =} \FloatTok{.247}\NormalTok{), }
                   \DataTypeTok{n1 =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{n2 =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sig.level =} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

     difference of proportion power calculation for binomial distribution (arcsine transformation) 

              h = 0.1796783
             n1 = 500
             n2 = 500
      sig.level = 0.05
          power = 0.8108416
    alternative = two.sided

NOTE: different sample sizes
\end{verbatim}

or we could instead have used\ldots{}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{power.prop.test}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{p1 =} \FloatTok{.174}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{p2 =} \FloatTok{.247}\NormalTok{, }
                \DataTypeTok{sig.level =} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

     Two-sample comparison of proportions power calculation 

              n = 500
             p1 = 0.174
             p2 = 0.247
      sig.level = 0.05
          power = 0.8091808
    alternative = two.sided

NOTE: n is number in *each* group
\end{verbatim}

Note that these two sample size estimation approaches are approximations, and use slightly different approaches, so it's not surprising that the answers are similar, but not completely identical.

\hypertarget{quiz-2-review}{%
\chapter{Quiz 2 Review}\label{quiz-2-review}}

There are 15 items here, and then a set of answer sketches follow the questions. This \textbf{isn't a complete review} - there are no questions here about regression models, and that will certainly show up on Quiz 2, as an example.

\hypertarget{review-items-1-7}{%
\section{Review Items 1-7}\label{review-items-1-7}}

Researchers comparing the effectiveness of two pain medications randomly selected a group of patients who had been complaining of a certain kind of joint pain. They randomly divided those people into two groups, then administered the medications. Of the 85 people in the group who received medication A, 65 said that it provided relief. Of the 70 people in the group receiving medication B, 45 reported that it provided relief.

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  Use the Agresti-Coull approach to specify a 95\% confidence interval for the proportion of people who find relief from this kind of joint pain by using medication A.
\item
  Now use the same approach to specify a 95\% confidence interval for the proportion of people who find relief using medication B.
\item
  Do the confidence intervals in items 1 and 2 overlap? What conclusions can you draw in light of that overlap (or lack thereof) about whether medication A or medication B is clearly more effective?
\item
  Specify and display the correct 2x2 table (incorporating a Bayesian augmentation) analysis to enable you to study the A - B difference in the true proportions of people who find these medications effective.
\item
  Use the 2x2 table results to specify an appropriate odds ratio and its 95\% confidence interval in this situation, and explain what the values mean in context.
\item
  Specify the hypotheses (\(H_0\) and \(H_A\)) tested by the Fisher exact test you obtain in your 2x2 table. What does the provided \emph{p} value tell you about what conclusion you should draw in this case regarding those hypotheses?
\item
  If you have made an error in your conclusion for item 6, was it a Type I error or a Type II error? How do you know?
\end{enumerate}

\hypertarget{review-items-8-9}{%
\section{Review Items 8-9}\label{review-items-8-9}}

For each of the following statements, indicate whether or not the statement is true or false, and specify how you know.

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{7}
\tightlist
\item
  If there is sufficient evidence to reject a null hypothesis at the 10\% level, then there is sufficient evidence to reject it at the 5\% level.
\item
  A sample histogram will follow a normal distribution if the sample size is large enough.
\end{enumerate}

\hypertarget{review-items-10-13}{%
\section{Review Items 10-13}\label{review-items-10-13}}

Charles Darwin carried out an experiment to study whether seedlings from cross-fertilized plants tend to be superior to those from self-fertilized plants. He covered a number of plants with fine netting so that insects would be unable to fertilize them. He fertilized a number of flowers on each plant with their own pollen and he fertilized an equal number of flowers on the same plant with pollen from a distant plant. (He did not specify how he decided which flowers received which treatments.) The seeds from the flowers were allowed to ripen and were set in wet sand to germinate. He placed two seedlings of the same age in a pot, one from a seed from a self-fertilized flower and one from a cross-fertilized flower.

He repeated this process with a total of 15 such pots. Each pot was then set aside for a time, so that the two plants in the plot would receive similar exposure to atmospheric conditions (sun, rainfall, etc.). Later, he gathered the heights of the plants (in inches) that came from those 15 cross-fertilized and 15 self-fertilized seeds at certain points in time. Those data are contained in the \texttt{darwin.csv} data set on our course website.

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{9}
\tightlist
\item
  Does this study call for a paired samples or independent samples comparison? How do you know?
\item
  Display and interpret an appropriate graph to determine whether a t-test or a Wilcoxon test would be more appropriate for these data.
\item
  Use the method (t or Wilcoxon) you specified in item 11 to find an appropriate 95\% confidence interval for the average height difference between cross-fertilized and self-fertilized seedlings. Verify that your confidence interval describes the ``cross'' - ``self'' difference, rather than the opposite direction.
\item
  Use an appropriate bootstrap procedure (setting your random seed to be \texttt{4310}) to provide an alternative answer for the question posed in item 12. Is this bootstrap confidence interval wider or narrower than the interval you produced in item 12?
\end{enumerate}

\hypertarget{review-items-14-15}{%
\section{Review Items 14-15}\label{review-items-14-15}}

You have been asked how large a sample size will be required for a clinical trial comparing two different approaches to blood pressure control. In approach A, we believe that the average systolic blood pressure will drop by 7 mm Hg, on the basis of our prior work in this area, while in the new approach B, we hope to see a clinically meaningful additional decline - specifically, we are looking for at least a 50\% larger decline, so that the average systolic blood pressure will drop by 10.5 mm Hg or more over the same amount of time. Thus, the minimum clinically meaningful difference we are looking for is 3.5 mm Hg. Suppose we believe that the relevant standard deviation is 9 mm Hg, and we want to complete the trial using a 5\% significance level and a two-sided t test.

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{13}
\tightlist
\item
  What will be the power of the test if we have a balanced design with 120 subjects in approach A and 120 different subjects in approach B? Show your calculation, and state your final result in a sentence.
\item
  What is the smallest total sample size that we can use in a balanced design to maintain at least 90\% power to detect the difference of interest, while still using independent samples? Show your calculation, and state your final result in a sentence.
\end{enumerate}

\hypertarget{answer-sketch-for-review-items}{%
\section{Answer Sketch for Review Items}\label{answer-sketch-for-review-items}}

\hypertarget{answer-1}{%
\subsection{Answer 1}\label{answer-1}}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mosaic}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{binom.test}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =} \DecValTok{65}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{85}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{ci.method =} \StringTok{"agresti-coull"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

	Exact binomial test (Agresti-Coull CI)

data:  65 out of 85
number of successes = 65, number of trials = 85, p-value = 1.03e-06
alternative hypothesis: true probability of success is not equal to 0.5
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.6636138 0.8429064
sample estimates:
probability of success 
             0.7647059 
\end{verbatim}

The 95\% confidence interval for the proportion of people using medication A who obtain relief is (0.664, 0.843). We are 95\% confident that the true percentage of people who find relief using medication A is between 66.4\% and 84.3\%.

\hypertarget{answer-2}{%
\subsection{Answer 2}\label{answer-2}}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mosaic}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{binom.test}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =} \DecValTok{45}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{70}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{ci.method =} \StringTok{"agresti-coull"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

	Exact binomial test (Agresti-Coull CI)

data:  45 out of 70
number of successes = 45, number of trials = 70, p-value = 0.02246
alternative hypothesis: true probability of success is not equal to 0.5
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.5256452 0.7452053
sample estimates:
probability of success 
             0.6428571 
\end{verbatim}

The 95\% confidence interval for the proportion of people using medication B who obtain relief is (0.526, 0.745).

\hypertarget{answer-3}{%
\subsection{Answer 3}\label{answer-3}}

The confidence intervals do overlap, so we cannot conclude from the separate intervals that there is (or isn't) a statistically significant difference in the effectiveness rates for medications A and B. If the confidence intervals didn't overlap, then we would know that there was a statistically significant difference in effectiveness between the two medications.

\hypertarget{answer-4}{%
\subsection{Answer 4}\label{answer-4}}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{twobytwo}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{65}\OperatorTok{+}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{20}\OperatorTok{+}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{45}\OperatorTok{+}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{25}\OperatorTok{+}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Med. A"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Med. B"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Relief"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"No Relief"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
2 by 2 table analysis: 
------------------------------------------------------ 
Outcome   : Relief 
Comparing : Med. A vs. Med. B 

       Relief No Relief    P(Relief) 95% conf. interval
Med. A     67        22       0.7528    0.6530   0.8314
Med. B     47        27       0.6351    0.5202   0.7365

                                   95% conf. interval
             Relative Risk: 1.1853    0.9610   1.4619
         Sample Odds Ratio: 1.7495    0.8906   3.4370
Conditional MLE Odds Ratio: 1.7435    0.8434   3.6398
    Probability difference: 0.1177   -0.0233   0.2556

             Exact P-value: 0.1234 
        Asymptotic P-value: 0.1045 
------------------------------------------------------
\end{verbatim}

\hypertarget{answer-5}{%
\subsection{Answer 5}\label{answer-5}}

The odds ratio is 1.75, with 95\% confidence interval (0.89, 3.44). The point estimate states that the odds of finding relief with medication A are 75\% higher than the odds of finding relief with medication B. But the confidence interval indicates that, with 95\% confidence, we can conclude only that the odds of relief with medication A are between 0.89 and 3.44 times as high as the odds of relief with medication B. Note that 1 is included in that interval.

\hypertarget{answer-6}{%
\subsection{Answer 6}\label{answer-6}}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \(H_0\): Medication Choice (A or B) is unrelated to the probability of Relief
\item
  \(H_A\): Medication Choice and Relief are associated
\end{itemize}

The \emph{p} value is 0.12, from the Fisher exact test. This means that we must retain the null hypothesis, and we cannot conclude that there is a detectable association between Medication choice and the probability of Relief based on this sample.

\hypertarget{answer-7}{%
\subsection{Answer 7}\label{answer-7}}

You would have made a Type II error. A Type II error can be made if you incorrectly retain \(H_0\). Since we retain \(H_0\), if we've made an error, it must have been a Type II error, since a Type I error occurs when you incorrectly reject \(H_0\).

\hypertarget{answer-8}{%
\subsection{Answer 8}\label{answer-8}}

This is FALSE. Sufficient evidence to reject \(H_0\) at the 10\% level means that we have a \emph{p} value \textless{} 0.10. In order to have sufficient evidence to reject \(H_0\) at the 5\% level, we'd need to have a \emph{p} value \textless{} 0.05. If our \emph{p} \textless{} 0.10, this doesn't guarantee that it is also true that \emph{p} \textless{} 0.05.

\hypertarget{answer-9}{%
\subsection{Answer 9}\label{answer-9}}

Also FALSE. The mean of a sample will approach a Normal distribution, but if the data are skewed, the data will still be skewed no matter how many observations we see.

\hypertarget{answer-10}{%
\subsection{Answer 10}\label{answer-10}}

These samples are paired by the pot. Each pot provides a cross-fertilized seedling height and a self-fertilized seedling height. We should be comparing paired differences.

\hypertarget{answer-11}{%
\subsection{Answer 11}\label{answer-11}}

We need a plot of the 15 paired differences, for example a boxplot, or a normal Q-Q plot.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{darwin <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{read_csv}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"data/darwin.csv"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{clean_names}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Parsed with column specification:
cols(
  pair = col_double(),
  `cross-fertilized` = col_double(),
  `self-fertilized` = col_double()
)
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{darwin <-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{darwin }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{diffs =}\NormalTok{ cross_fertilized }\OperatorTok{-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{self_fertilized)}

\NormalTok{p1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(darwin, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ diffs)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_violin}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_boxplot}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{width =} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"forestgreen"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{outlier.color =} \StringTok{"forestgreen"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Boxplot"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Difference in Seedling Height (inches)"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{""}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p2 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(darwin, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{sample =}\NormalTok{ diffs)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_qq}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"forestgreen"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_qq_line}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Normal Q-Q"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Difference in Seedling Height (inches)"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{""}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p1 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p2 }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{plot_annotation}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Cross-Self Fertilized Difference in Seedling Height"}\NormalTok{,}
                  \DataTypeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"in inches for 15 pairs of seedlings"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/a11-1.pdf}

It appears that we have two low outliers out of the 15 paired differences. Assuming normality seems inappropriate here. I would probably use a Wilcoxon approach instead.

\hypertarget{answer-12}{%
\subsection{Answer 12}\label{answer-12}}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{wilcox.test}\NormalTok{(darwin}\OperatorTok{$}\NormalTok{diffs, }\DataTypeTok{conf.int=}\OtherTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{conf.level=}\FloatTok{0.95}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

	Wilcoxon signed rank exact test

data:  darwin$diffs
V = 96, p-value = 0.04126
alternative hypothesis: true location is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.5000 5.1875
sample estimates:
(pseudo)median 
         3.125 
\end{verbatim}

The cross-self differences appear to have a population pseudomedian which we are 95\% confident is between 0.5 and 5.2 inches. The cross-fertilized plants appear to be taller on average than the self-fertilized plants in the same pot.

\hypertarget{answer-13}{%
\subsection{Answer 13}\label{answer-13}}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{4310}\NormalTok{); Hmisc}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{smean.cl.boot}\NormalTok{(darwin}\OperatorTok{$}\NormalTok{diffs)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
    Mean    Lower    Upper 
2.616667 0.316250 4.758958 
\end{verbatim}

This confidence interval is a bit narrower than the interval in item 12, and also shifted a bit closer to zero. We are 95\% confident that the population mean cross-self difference is between 0.3 and 4.8 inches.

\hypertarget{answer-14}{%
\subsection{Answer 14}\label{answer-14}}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{power.t.test}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{delta =} \FloatTok{3.5}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sd =} \DecValTok{9}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sig.level =} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

     Two-sample t test power calculation 

              n = 120
          delta = 3.5
             sd = 9
      sig.level = 0.05
          power = 0.8508717
    alternative = two.sided

NOTE: n is number in *each* group
\end{verbatim}

Such a test will have just over 85\% power to detect the specified minimum clinically meaningful difference of 3.5 mm Hg, using a 5\% two-sided significance level.

\hypertarget{answer-15}{%
\subsection{Answer 15}\label{answer-15}}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{power.t.test}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{power=}\FloatTok{0.9}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{delta =} \FloatTok{3.5}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sd =} \DecValTok{9}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sig.level =} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

     Two-sample t test power calculation 

              n = 139.9227
          delta = 3.5
             sd = 9
      sig.level = 0.05
          power = 0.9
    alternative = two.sided

NOTE: n is number in *each* group
\end{verbatim}

The minimum sample size we'll need is 140 subjects in each approach (A and B), so that's a total sample size of 280, to achieve 90\% or higher power for the specified test while still using independent samples.

\hypertarget{missing-data-mechanisms-and-simple-imputation}{%
\chapter{Missing Data Mechanisms and Simple Imputation}\label{missing-data-mechanisms-and-simple-imputation}}

Almost all serious statistical analyses have to deal with missing data. Data values that are missing are indicated in R, and to R, by the symbol \texttt{NA}.

\hypertarget{adding-two-new-packages}{%
\section{Adding Two New Packages}\label{adding-two-new-packages}}

We'll use the \texttt{naniar} and \texttt{simputation} packages in this work.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{library}\NormalTok{(naniar)}
\KeywordTok{library}\NormalTok{(simputation)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\hypertarget{simulated-example-with-15-subjects}{%
\section{Simulated Example with 15 subjects}\label{simulated-example-with-15-subjects}}

In the following tiny data set called \texttt{sbp\_example}, we have four variables for a set of 15 subjects. In addition to a subject id, we have:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  the treatment this subject received (A, B or C are the treatments),
\item
  an indicator (1 = yes, 0 = no) of whether the subject has diabetes,
\item
  the subject's systolic blood pressure at baseline
\item
  the subject's systolic blood pressure after the application of the treatment
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{# create some temporary variables}
\NormalTok{subject <-}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{101}\OperatorTok{:}\DecValTok{115}
