\NormalTok{x1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"A"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"B"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"C"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"A"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"C"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"A"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"A"}\NormalTok{, }\OtherTok{NA}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"B"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"C"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"A"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"B"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"C"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"A"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"B"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{x2 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\OtherTok{NA}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\OtherTok{NA}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\OtherTok{NA}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{x3 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{145}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{150}\NormalTok{, }\OtherTok{NA}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{155}\NormalTok{, }\OtherTok{NA}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{135}\NormalTok{, }\OtherTok{NA}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{115}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{170}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{150}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{145}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{140}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{160}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{135}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{x4 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{105}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{135}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{150}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{135}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{115}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{160}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{150}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{130}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{155}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{140}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{140}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{150}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{135}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{sbp_example <-}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(subject, }\DataTypeTok{treat =} \KeywordTok{factor}\NormalTok{(x1), }\DataTypeTok{diabetes =}\NormalTok{ x2, }
         \DataTypeTok{sbp.before =}\NormalTok{ x3, }\DataTypeTok{sbp.after =}\NormalTok{ x4) }

\KeywordTok{rm}\NormalTok{(subject, x1, x2, x3, x4) }\CommentTok{# just cleaning up}

\NormalTok{sbp_example}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 15 x 5
   subject treat diabetes sbp.before sbp.after
     <int> <fct>    <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>
 1     101 A            1        120       105
 2     102 B            0        145       135
 3     103 C            0        150       150
 4     104 A            1         NA       120
 5     105 C           NA        155       135
 6     106 A            1         NA       115
 7     107 A            0        135       160
 8     108 <NA>         1         NA       150
 9     109 B           NA        115       130
10     110 C            1        170       155
11     111 A            0        150       140
12     112 B            0        145       140
13     113 C            1        140       150
14     114 A            1        160       135
15     115 B           NA        135       120
\end{verbatim}

\hypertarget{identifying-missingness-with-naniar-functions}{%
\section{\texorpdfstring{Identifying missingness with \texttt{naniar} functions}{Identifying missingness with naniar functions}}\label{identifying-missingness-with-naniar-functions}}

The \texttt{naniar} package has many useful functions.

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  How many missing values do we have, overall?
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{n_miss}\NormalTok{(sbp_example)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] 7
\end{verbatim}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{1}
\tightlist
\item
  How many variables have missing values, overall?
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{n_var_miss}\NormalTok{(sbp_example)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] 3
\end{verbatim}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{2}
\tightlist
\item
  Which variables contain missing values?
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{miss_var_which}\NormalTok{(sbp_example)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "treat"      "diabetes"   "sbp.before"
\end{verbatim}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{3}
\tightlist
\item
  How many missing values do we have in each variable?
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{miss_var_summary}\NormalTok{(sbp_example)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 5 x 3
  variable   n_miss pct_miss
  <chr>       <int>    <dbl>
1 diabetes        3    20   
2 sbp.before      3    20   
3 treat           1     6.67
4 subject         0     0   
5 sbp.after       0     0   
\end{verbatim}

We are missing one \texttt{treat}, 3 \texttt{diabetes} and 3 \texttt{sbp.before} values.

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{4}
\tightlist
\item
  Can we plot missingness, by variable?
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{gg_miss_var}\NormalTok{(sbp_example)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-296-1.pdf}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{5}
\tightlist
\item
  How many of the cases (rows) have missing values?
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{n_case_miss}\NormalTok{(sbp_example)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] 6
\end{verbatim}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{6}
\tightlist
\item
  How many cases have complete data, with no missing values?
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{n_case_complete}\NormalTok{(sbp_example)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] 9
\end{verbatim}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{7}
\tightlist
\item
  Can we tabulate missingness by case?
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{miss_case_table}\NormalTok{(sbp_example)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 3 x 3
  n_miss_in_case n_cases pct_cases
           <int>   <int>     <dbl>
1              0       9     60   
2              1       5     33.3 
3              2       1      6.67
\end{verbatim}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{8}
\tightlist
\item
  Which cases have missing values?
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{miss_case_summary}\NormalTok{(sbp_example)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 15 x 3
    case n_miss pct_miss
   <int>  <int>    <dbl>
 1     8      2       40
 2     4      1       20
 3     5      1       20
 4     6      1       20
 5     9      1       20
 6    15      1       20
 7     1      0        0
 8     2      0        0
 9     3      0        0
10     7      0        0
11    10      0        0
12    11      0        0
13    12      0        0
14    13      0        0
15    14      0        0
\end{verbatim}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{9}
\tightlist
\item
  How can we identify the subjects with missing data?
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{sbp_example }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}\OperatorTok{!}\KeywordTok{complete.cases}\NormalTok{(.))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 6 x 5
  subject treat diabetes sbp.before sbp.after
    <int> <fct>    <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>
1     104 A            1         NA       120
2     105 C           NA        155       135
3     106 A            1         NA       115
4     108 <NA>         1         NA       150
5     109 B           NA        115       130
6     115 B           NA        135       120
\end{verbatim}

We have nine subjects with complete data, three subjects with missing \texttt{diabetes} (only), two subjects with missing \texttt{sbp.before} (only), and 1 subject who is missing both \texttt{treat} and \texttt{sbp.before}.

\hypertarget{missing-data-mechanisms}{%
\section{Missing-data mechanisms}\label{missing-data-mechanisms}}

My source for this description of mechanisms is Chapter 25 of \citet{Gelman-Hill}, and that chapter is \href{http://www.stat.columbia.edu/~gelman/arm/missing.pdf}{available at this link}.

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{MCAR = Missingness completely at random}. A variable is missing completely at random if the probability of missingness is the same for all units, for example, if for each subject, we decide whether to collect the \texttt{diabetes} status by rolling a die and refusing to answer if a ``6'' shows up. If data are missing completely at random, then throwing out cases with missing data does not bias your inferences.
\item
  \textbf{Missingness that depends only on observed predictors}. A more general assumption, called \textbf{missing at random} or \textbf{MAR}, is that the probability a variable is missing depends only on available information. Here, we would have to be willing to assume that the probability of nonresponse to \texttt{diabetes} depends only on the other, fully recorded variables in the data. It is often reasonable to model this process as a logistic regression, where the outcome variable equals 1 for observed cases and 0 for missing. When an outcome variable is missing at random, it is acceptable to exclude the missing cases (that is, to treat them as NA), as long as the regression controls for all the variables that affect the probability of missingness.
\item
  \textbf{Missingness that depends on unobserved predictors}. Missingness is no longer ``at random'' if it depends on information that has not been recorded and this information also predicts the missing values. If a particular treatment causes discomfort, a patient is more likely to drop out of the study. This missingness is not at random (unless ``discomfort'' is measured and observed for all patients). If missingness is not at random, it must be explicitly modeled, or else you must accept some bias in your inferences.
\item
  \textbf{Missingness that depends on the missing value itself.} Finally, a particularly difficult situation arises when the probability of missingness depends on the (potentially missing) variable itself. For example, suppose that people with higher earnings are less likely to reveal them.
\end{enumerate}

Essentially, situations 3 and 4 are referred to collectively as \textbf{non-random missingness}, and cause more trouble for us than 1 and 2.

\hypertarget{options-for-dealing-with-missingness}{%
\section{Options for Dealing with Missingness}\label{options-for-dealing-with-missingness}}

There are several available methods for dealing with missing data that are MCAR or MAR, but they basically boil down to:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Complete Case (or Available Case) analyses
\item
  Single Imputation
\item
  Multiple Imputation
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{complete-case-and-available-case-analyses}{%
\section{Complete Case (and Available Case) analyses}\label{complete-case-and-available-case-analyses}}

In \textbf{Complete Case} analyses, rows containing NA values are omitted from the data before analyses commence. This is the default approach for many statistical software packages, and may introduce unpredictable bias and fail to include some useful, often hard-won information.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  A complete case analysis can be appropriate when the number of missing observations is not large, and the missing pattern is either MCAR (missing completely at random) or MAR (missing at random.)
\item
  Two problems arise with complete-case analysis:

  \begin{enumerate}
  \def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
  \tightlist
  \item
    If the units with missing values differ systematically from the completely observed cases, this could bias the complete-case analysis.
  \item
    If many variables are included in a model, there may be very few complete cases, so that most of the data would be discarded for the sake of a straightforward analysis.
  \end{enumerate}
\item
  A related approach is \emph{available-case} analysis where different aspects of a problem are studied with different subsets of the data, perhaps identified on the basis of what is missing in them.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{single-imputation}{%
\section{Single Imputation}\label{single-imputation}}

In \textbf{single imputation} analyses, NA values are estimated/replaced \emph{one time} with \emph{one particular data value} for the purpose of obtaining more complete samples, at the expense of creating some potential bias in the eventual conclusions or obtaining slightly \emph{less} accurate estimates than would be available if there were no missing values in the data.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  A single imputation can be just a replacement with the mean or median (for a quantity) or the mode (for a categorical variable.) However, such an approach, though easy to understand, underestimates variance and ignores the relationship of missing values to other variables.
\item
  Single imputation can also be done using a variety of models to try to capture information about the NA values that are available in other variables within the data set.
\item
  The \texttt{simputation} package can help us execute single imputations using a wide variety of techniques, within the pipe approach used by the \texttt{tidyverse}. Another approach I have used in the past is the \texttt{mice} package, which can also perform single imputations.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{multiple-imputation}{%
\section{Multiple Imputation}\label{multiple-imputation}}

\textbf{Multiple imputation}, where NA values are repeatedly estimated/replaced with multiple data values, for the purpose of obtaining mode complete samples \emph{and} capturing details of the variation inherent in the fact that the data have missingness, so as to obtain \emph{more} accurate estimates than are possible with single imputation.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  We'll postpone further discussion of multiple imputation to 432.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{building-a-complete-case-analysis}{%
\section{Building a Complete Case Analysis}\label{building-a-complete-case-analysis}}

We can drop all of the missing values from a data set with \texttt{drop\_na} or with \texttt{na.omit} or by filtering for \texttt{complete.cases}. Any of these approaches produces the same result - a new data set with 9 rows (after dropping the six subjects with any NA values) and 5 columns.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{cc}\FloatTok{.1}\NormalTok{ <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{na.omit}\NormalTok{(sbp_example)}
\NormalTok{cc}\FloatTok{.2}\NormalTok{ <-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp_example }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{drop_na}
\NormalTok{cc}\FloatTok{.3}\NormalTok{ <-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp_example }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{complete.cases}\NormalTok{(.))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\hypertarget{single-imputation-with-the-mean-or-mode}{%
\section{Single Imputation with the Mean or Mode}\label{single-imputation-with-the-mean-or-mode}}

The most straightforward approach to single imputation is to impute a single summary of the variable, such as the mean, median or mode.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mosaic}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{favstats}\NormalTok{(}\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp.before, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ sbp_example)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
 min  Q1 median     Q3 max     mean       sd  n missing
 115 135    145 151.25 170 143.3333 15.71527 12       3
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{sbp_example }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{tabyl}\NormalTok{(diabetes, treat) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{adorn_totals}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{where =} \KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"row"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"col"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
 diabetes A B C NA_ Total
        0 2 2 1   0     5
        1 4 0 2   1     7
     <NA> 0 2 1   0     3
    Total 6 4 4   1    15
\end{verbatim}

Here, suppose we decide to impute

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \texttt{sbp.before} with the mean (143.3) among non-missing values,
\item
  \texttt{diabetes} with its more common value, 1, and
\item
  \texttt{treat} with its more common value, or mode (A)
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{si}\FloatTok{.1}\NormalTok{ <-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp_example }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{replace_na}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{sbp.before =} \FloatTok{143.33}\NormalTok{,}
                    \DataTypeTok{diabetes =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
                    \DataTypeTok{treat =} \StringTok{"A"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{si}\FloatTok{.1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 15 x 5
   subject treat diabetes sbp.before sbp.after
     <int> <fct>    <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>
 1     101 A            1       120        105
 2     102 B            0       145        135
 3     103 C            0       150        150
 4     104 A            1       143.       120
 5     105 C            1       155        135
 6     106 A            1       143.       115
 7     107 A            0       135        160
 8     108 A            1       143.       150
 9     109 B            1       115        130
10     110 C            1       170        155
11     111 A            0       150        140
12     112 B            0       145        140
13     113 C            1       140        150
14     114 A            1       160        135
15     115 B            1       135        120
\end{verbatim}

\hypertarget{doing-single-imputation-with-simputation}{%
\section{\texorpdfstring{Doing Single Imputation with \texttt{simputation}}{Doing Single Imputation with simputation}}\label{doing-single-imputation-with-simputation}}

Single imputation is a potentially appropriate method when missingness can be assumed to be either completely at random (MCAR) or dependent only on observed predictors (MAR). We'll use the \texttt{simputation} package to accomplish it.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The \texttt{simputation} vignette is available at \url{https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/simputation/vignettes/intro.html}
\item
  The \texttt{simputation} reference manual is available at \url{https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/simputation/simputation.pdf}
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{using-a-model-to-impute-sbp.before-and-diabetes}{%
\subsection{\texorpdfstring{Using a model to impute \texttt{sbp.before} and \texttt{diabetes}}{Using a model to impute sbp.before and diabetes}}\label{using-a-model-to-impute-sbp.before-and-diabetes}}

Suppose we wanted to use:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  a robust linear model to predict \texttt{sbp.before} missing values, on the basis of \texttt{sbp.after} and \texttt{diabetes} status, and
\item
  a predictive mean matching approach (which, unlike the robust linear model, will ensure that only values of \texttt{diabetes} that we've seen before will be imputed) to predict \texttt{diabetes} status, on the basis of \texttt{sbp.after}, and
\item
  a decision tree approach to predict \texttt{treat} status, using all other variables in the data
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{50001}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{imp}\FloatTok{.2}\NormalTok{ <-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp_example }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{impute_rlm}\NormalTok{(sbp.before }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp.after }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{diabetes) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{impute_pmm}\NormalTok{(diabetes }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp.after) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{impute_cart}\NormalTok{(treat }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{.)}

\NormalTok{imp}\FloatTok{.2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 15 x 5
   subject treat diabetes sbp.before sbp.after
 *   <int> <fct>    <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>
 1     101 A            1       120        105
 2     102 B            0       145        135
 3     103 C            0       150        150
 4     104 A            1       139.       120
 5     105 C            1       155        135
 6     106 A            1       136.       115
 7     107 A            0       135        160
 8     108 A            1       155.       150
 9     109 B            1       115        130
10     110 C            1       170        155
11     111 A            0       150        140
12     112 B            0       145        140
13     113 C            1       140        150
14     114 A            1       160        135
15     115 B            1       135        120
\end{verbatim}

Details on the many available methods in \texttt{simputation} are provided \href{https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/simputation/simputation.pdf}{in its manual}. These include:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \texttt{impute\_cart} uses a Classification and Regression Tree approach for numerical or categorical data. There is also an \texttt{impute\_rf} command which uses Random Forests for imputation.
\item
  \texttt{impute\_pmm} is one of several ``hot deck'' options for imputation, this one is predictive mean matching, which can be used with numeric data (only). Missing values are first imputed using a predictive model. Next, these predictions are replaced with the observed values which are nearest to the prediction. Other imputation options in this group include random hot deck, sequential hot deck and k-nearest neighbor imputation.
\item
  \texttt{impute\_rlm} is one of several regression imputation methods, including linear models, robust linear models (which use what is called M-estimation to impute numerical variables) and lasso/elastic net/ridge regression models.
\end{itemize}

The \texttt{simputation} package can also do EM-based multivariate imputation, and multivariate random forest imputation, and several other approaches.

\hypertarget{part-part-c.-building-models}{%
\part*{Part C. Building Models}\label{part-part-c.-building-models}}
\addcontentsline{toc}{part}{Part C. Building Models}

\hypertarget{multiple-regression-introduction}{%
\chapter{Multiple Regression: Introduction}\label{multiple-regression-introduction}}

In Chapter \ref{Hydrate-Study} in working with a study of dehydration recovery in children, we discussed many of the fundamental ideas of multiple regression. There, we provided code and insight into the scatterplot and the scatterplot matrix, fit linear models and plotted the coefficients, analyzed summary output from \texttt{summary}, \texttt{tidy} and \texttt{glance} as well as the ANOVA table, and plotted residuals vs.~fitted values.

In the remaining chapters, we will build on that foundation in three additional examples.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The \texttt{wcgs} data from the Western Collaborative Group Study, which we described and studied back in Chapter \ref{WCGS-Study}.
\item
  The \texttt{emp\_bmi} data from a study published in \emph{BMJ Open} of a nationally representative sample of over 7000 participants in the Korean Longitudinal Study of Aging.
\item
  The \texttt{gala} data, which describe features of the 30 Galapagos Islands.
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{wcgs <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{read_csv}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"data/wcgs.csv"}\NormalTok{) }
\NormalTok{emp_bmi <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{read_csv}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"data/emp_bmi.csv"}\NormalTok{) }
\NormalTok{gala <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{read_csv}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"data/gala.csv"}\NormalTok{) }
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\hypertarget{add-some-packages}{%
\section{Add Some Packages}\label{add-some-packages}}

Here, we'll add several packages to those we've loaded at the start of the notes, and, as always, finish up with the \texttt{broom} and \texttt{tidyverse} packages.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{library}\NormalTok{(naniar)}
\KeywordTok{library}\NormalTok{(simputation)}
\KeywordTok{library}\NormalTok{(car)}
\KeywordTok{library}\NormalTok{(GGally)}
\KeywordTok{library}\NormalTok{(ggrepel)}
\KeywordTok{library}\NormalTok{(broom)}
\KeywordTok{library}\NormalTok{(tidyverse)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\hypertarget{reminders-of-a-few-key-concepts}{%
\section{Reminders of a few Key Concepts}\label{reminders-of-a-few-key-concepts}}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Scatterplots} We have often accompanied our scatterplots with regression lines estimated by the method of least squares, and by loess smooths which permit local polynomial functions to display curved relationships, and occasionally presented in the form of a scatterplot matrix to enable simultaneous comparisons of multiple two-way associations.
\item
  \textbf{Measures of Correlation/Association} By far the most commonly used is the Pearson correlation, which is a unitless (scale-free) measure of bivariate linear association for the variables X and Y, symbolized by r, and ranging from -1 to +1. The Pearson correlation is a function of the slope of the least squares regression line, divided by the product of the standard deviations of X and Y. We have also mentioned the \emph{Spearman} rank correlation coefficient, which is obtained by using the usual formula for a Pearson correlation, but on the ranks (1 = minimum, n = maximum, with average ranks are applied to the ties) of the X and Y values. This approach (running a correlation of the orderings of the data) substantially reduces the effect of outliers. The result still ranges from -1 to +1, with 0 indicating no linear association.
\item
  \textbf{Fitting Linear Models} We have fit several styles of linear model to date, including both \emph{simple} regressions, where our outcome Y is modeled as a linear function of a single predictor X, and \emph{multiple} regression models, where more than one predictor is used. Functions from the \texttt{broom} package can be used to yield several crucial results, in addition to those we can obtain with a \texttt{summary} of the model. These include:
\end{enumerate}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  (from \texttt{tidy}) the estimated coefficients (intercept and slope(s)) of the fitted model, and
\item
  (from \texttt{glance}) the \(R^2\) or coefficient of determination, which specifies the proportion of variation in our outcome accounted for by the linear model, and various other summaries of the model's quality of fit
\item
  (from \texttt{augment}) fitted values, residuals and other summaries related to individual points used to fit the model, or individual predictions made by the model, which will be helpful for assessing predictive accuracy and for developing diagnostic tools for assessing the assumptions of multiple regression.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{what-is-important-in-431}{%
\section{What is important in 431?}\label{what-is-important-in-431}}

In 431, my primary goal is to immerse you in several cases, which will demonstrate good statistical practice in the analysis of data using multiple regression models. Often, we will leave gaps for 432, but the principal goal is to get you to the point where you can do a solid (if not quite complete) analysis of data for the modeling part (Study 2) of Project B.

Key topics regarding multiple regression we cover in 431 include:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  Describing the multivariate relationship
  - Scatterplots and smoothing
  - Correlation coefficients, Correlation matrices
\item
  Transformations and Re-expression
  - The need for transformation
  - Using a Box-Cox method to help identify effective transformation choices
  - Measuring and addressing collinearity
\item
  Testing the significance of a multiple regression model
  - T tests for individual predictors as last predictor in
  - Global F tests based on ANOVA to assess overall predictive significance
  - Incremental and Sequential testing of groups of predictors
\item
  Interpreting the predictive value of a model
  - \(R^2\) and Adjusted \(R^2\), along with AIC and BIC
  - Residual standard deviation and RMSE
\item
  Checking model assumptions
  - Residual Analysis including studentized residuals, and the major plots
  - Identifying points with high Leverage
  - Assessing Influence numerically and graphically
\item
  Model Selection
  - The importance of parsimony
  - Stepwise regression
\item
  Assessing Predictive Accuracy through Cross-Validation
  - Summaries of predictive error
  - Plotting predictions across multiple models
\item
  Summarizing the Key Findings of the Model, briefly and accurately
  - Making the distinction between causal findings and associations
  - The importance of logic, theory and empirical evidence. (LTE)
\end{enumerate}

\hypertarget{Reg-Diag}{%
\chapter{Regression Diagnostics}\label{Reg-Diag}}

Some of this discussion comes from \citet{BockVD}.

Multiple linear regression (also called ordinary least squares, or OLS) has four main assumptions that are derived from its model.

For a simple regression, the model underlying the regression line is \(E(Y) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x\)

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  where E(Y) = the expectation (mean) of the outcome Y,
\item
  \(\beta_0\) is the intercept, and
\item
  \(\beta_1\) is the slope
\end{itemize}

Now, if we have a multiple regression with three predictors \(x_1, x_2\) and \(x_3\), then the model becomes \(E(Y) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \beta_3 x_3\).

Alternatively, we can write the model to relate individual y's to the x's by adding an individual error term: \(y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \beta_3 x_3 + \epsilon\)

Of course, the multiple regression model is not limited to three predictors. We will often use \emph{k} to represent the number of coefficients (slopes plus intercept) in the model, and will also use \emph{p} to represent the number of predictors (usually \emph{p} = \emph{k} - 1).

\hypertarget{building-an-example-500-subjects-from-wcgs}{%
\section{Building an Example: 500 subjects from WCGS}\label{building-an-example-500-subjects-from-wcgs}}

To fix ideas, suppose we return to the Western Collaborative Group Study (WCGS) data we studied back in Chapter \ref{WCGS-Study}, which described 3154 men ages 39-59 who were free of heart disease and were employees of one of 10 California companies in 1960-1961.

We are going to select a random sample of 500 of those men, with complete data on our outcome of interest, which is total cholesterol in mg/dl (\texttt{chol}) and on our three predictors of interest, which are systolic blood pressure in mm Hg (\texttt{sbp}), body mass index (\texttt{bmi}) and behavioral pattern (\texttt{dibpat}), which is a two-category variable with levels ``Type A'' or ``Type B''.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Individuals exhibiting a Type A behavior pattern show evidence of being competitive and self-critical without feeling joy in their efforts or accomplishments, and are more likely to have high work involvement, important life imbalance, and high blood pressure.
\item
  Individuals exhibiting a Type B behavior pattern show evidence of being more reflective, more relaxed, less competitive and less anxious then Type A individuals.
\item
  There is substantial evidence to discredit the Type A Behavior Pattern theory in some areas, and some scholars definitely argue that Type A behavior is not a good predictor of coronary heart disease.
\end{itemize}

Below we create this sample of 500 subjects with complete data on our outcome and each of our three predictors.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{12345}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{wcgs_}\DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{ <-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{wcgs }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id, sbp, bmi, dibpat, chol) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{complete.cases}\NormalTok{(.)) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{slice_sample}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{replace =} \OtherTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Next, we'll look at a scatterplot matrix, placing our outcome last, as usual.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{# requires library(GGally)}

\KeywordTok{ggpairs}\NormalTok{(wcgs_}\DecValTok{500} \OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(sbp, bmi, dibpat, chol),}
        \DataTypeTok{lower =} \KeywordTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{combo =} \KeywordTok{wrap}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"facethist"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{binwidth =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)), }
        \DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Scatterplot Matrix for 500 WCGS subjects"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/spm_wcgs_500-1.pdf}

Next, we create our planned linear model, and evaluate the coefficients and fit quality.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{chol_m1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(chol }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{bmi }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dibpat, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_}\DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{)}

\KeywordTok{summary}\NormalTok{(chol_m1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

Call:
lm(formula = chol ~ sbp + bmi + dibpat, data = wcgs_500)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-113.133  -25.689   -1.932   24.036  186.390 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  214.5185    19.8825  10.789  < 2e-16 ***
sbp            0.2361     0.1125   2.099  0.03634 *  
bmi           -0.5894     0.7290  -0.808  0.41921    
dibpatType B -11.0408     3.5643  -3.098  0.00206 ** 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 39.66 on 496 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.02954,	Adjusted R-squared:  0.02367 
F-statistic: 5.033 on 3 and 496 DF,  p-value: 0.001915
\end{verbatim}

As usual, we'll use \texttt{tidy} and \texttt{glance} from the \texttt{broom} package (part of \texttt{tidymodels}) to evaluate the coefficients in more detail, and summarize the quality of fit for our model.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{tidy}\NormalTok{(chol_m1, }\DataTypeTok{conf.int =} \OtherTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{conf.level =} \FloatTok{0.90}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(term, estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{dig =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tabular}{l|r|r|r|r|r}
\hline
term & estimate & std.error & conf.low & conf.high & p.value\\
\hline
(Intercept) & 214.519 & 19.883 & 181.753 & 247.284 & 0.000\\
\hline
sbp & 0.236 & 0.112 & 0.051 & 0.421 & 0.036\\
\hline
bmi & -0.589 & 0.729 & -1.791 & 0.612 & 0.419\\
\hline
dibpatType B & -11.041 & 3.564 & -16.915 & -5.167 & 0.002\\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{glance}\NormalTok{(chol_m1) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(r.squared, adj.r.squared, sigma, AIC, BIC, nobs) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{dig =} \KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tabular}{r|r|r|r|r|r}
\hline
r.squared & adj.r.squared & sigma & AIC & BIC & nobs\\
\hline
0.03 & 0.024 & 39.7 & 5105.3 & 5126.4 & 500\\
\hline
\end{tabular}

This isn't a terrific model, accounting for only 3\% (according to \(R^2\)) of the variation in our outcome, although some of the individual predictors show effects that meet our usual standards for ``detectable'' results. Our goal, though, is to use this model as an example for studying residual diagnostics to search for possible violations of regression assumptions.

To assess regression assumptions, we'll have to look at residuals and fits for each of the individual points, which we can obtain use the \texttt{augment} function from the \texttt{broom} package.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{aug_chol1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{augment}\NormalTok{(chol_m1, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_}\DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This augments the original data with several summaries of the quality of fit for our individual rows in the \texttt{wcgs\_500} data. Here are the first few values for some of the elements of \texttt{augment}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{head}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id}\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{.resid)) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{dig =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tabular}{r|r|r|l|r|r|r}
\hline
id & sbp & bmi & dibpat & chol & .fitted & .resid\\
\hline
10155 & 120 & 18.162 & Type B & 294 & 221.104 & 72.896\\
\hline
3495 & 140 & 24.807 & Type A & 301 & 232.950 & 68.050\\
\hline
12634 & 124 & 21.616 & Type A & 196 & 231.054 & -35.054\\
\hline
21354 & 114 & 25.381 & Type A & 176 & 226.474 & -50.474\\
\hline
3085 & 190 & 22.349 & Type A & 241 & 246.203 & -5.203\\
\hline
3589 & 140 & 23.054 & Type B & 268 & 222.943 & 45.057\\
\hline
\end{tabular}

This includes the original data, plus the model's predicted value for \texttt{chol}, stored in \texttt{.fitted} and the residual (actual \texttt{chol} - \texttt{.fitted}) stored in \texttt{.resid}. Here are the other elements of the \texttt{augment} result.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{head}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id, .std.resid}\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{.cooksd)) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{dig =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tabular}{r|r|r|r|r}
\hline
id & .std.resid & .hat & .sigma & .cooksd\\
\hline
10155 & 1.854 & 0.017 & 39.563 & 0.015\\
\hline
3495 & 1.720 & 0.005 & 39.583 & 0.003\\
\hline
12634 & -0.887 & 0.007 & 39.670 & 0.001\\
\hline
21354 & -1.277 & 0.007 & 39.636 & 0.003\\
\hline
3085 & -0.134 & 0.038 & 39.700 & 0.000\\
\hline
3589 & 1.140 & 0.007 & 39.649 & 0.002\\
\hline
\end{tabular}

Here, we see:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \texttt{.std.resid} the standardized residual, which is a measure of how poorly fit the point is, scaled to have standard deviation 1 (the raw residuals, as well as these, already have mean 0)
\item
  \texttt{.hat}, the hat matrix value, which is a measurement of the \textbf{leverage} this point has on the model. Leverage is, essentially, a measure of how unusual the point is in terms of the predictors.
\item
  \texttt{.sigma}, the value of the residual standard error \texttt{.sigma} that would emerge if this point was deleted from the \texttt{chol\_m1} model, and
\item
  \texttt{.cooksd}, Cook's distance, or Cook's d, which measures the \textbf{influence} of this point on the model. A highly influential point (one with \texttt{.cooksd} greater than 0.5, for instance) would be a point that, if removed from the \texttt{chol\_m1} model, would substantially change the fit of the model or the coefficient estimates.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{the-four-key-regression-assumptions}{%
\section{The Four Key Regression Assumptions}\label{the-four-key-regression-assumptions}}

The assumptions and conditions for a multiple regression model are nearly the same as those for simple regression. The key assumptions that must be checked in a multiple regression model are:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Linearity Assumption
\item
  Independence Assumption
\item
  Equal Variance (Constant Variance / Homoscedasticity) Assumption
\item
  Normality Assumption
\end{itemize}

Happily, R is well suited to provide us with multiple diagnostic tools to generate plots and other summaries that will let us look more closely at each of these assumptions.

\hypertarget{the-linearity-assumption}{%
\section{The Linearity Assumption}\label{the-linearity-assumption}}

We are fitting a linear model.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  By \emph{linear}, we mean that each predictor value, x, appears simply multiplied by its coefficient and added to the model.
\item
  No x appears in an exponent or some other more complicated function.
\item
  If the regression model is true, then the outcome \emph{y} is linearly related to each of the \emph{x}'s.
\end{itemize}

Unfortunately, assuming the model is true is not sufficient to prove that the linear model fits, so we check what \citet{BockVD} call the ``Straight Enough Condition''

\hypertarget{initial-scatterplots-for-the-straight-enough-condition}{%
\subsection{Initial Scatterplots for the ``Straight Enough'' Condition}\label{initial-scatterplots-for-the-straight-enough-condition}}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Scatterplots of y against each of the predictors are reasonably straight.
\item
  The scatterplots need not show a strong (or any!) slope; we just check that there isn't a bend or other nonlinearity.
\item
  Any substantial curve is indicative of a potential problem.
\item
  Modest bends are not usually worthy of serious attention.
\end{itemize}

For example, in the \texttt{wcgs\_500} data, here are the relevant scatterplots for the quantitative predictors (in practice, I would simply look at the scatterplot matrix shown earlier) as well as a boxplot with violin for the categorical predictor (\texttt{dibpat}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(wcgs_}\DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ sbp, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ chol)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =} \OtherTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"lm"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =} \OtherTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"tomato"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x)}

\NormalTok{p2 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(wcgs_}\DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ bmi, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ chol)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =} \OtherTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"lm"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =} \OtherTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"tomato"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x)}

\NormalTok{p3 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(wcgs_}\DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ dibpat, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ chol)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_violin}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ dibpat)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_boxplot}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{width =} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{coord_flip}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{guides}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{fill =} \OtherTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{(p1 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p2) }\OperatorTok{/}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p3 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{plot_layout}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{heights =} \KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{plot_annotation}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"WCGS_500 Cholesterol vs. Predictors"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-314-1.pdf}

Here, I've simply placed \texttt{recov.score} on the vertical (Y) axis, plotted against each of the predictors, in turn. I've added a straight line (OLS) fit {[}in tomato red{]} and a loess smooth {[}in blue{]} to each plot to guide your assessment of the ``straight enough'' condition. Note the use here of \texttt{span\ =\ 2} in the \texttt{loess} smooths to produce lines that are less wiggly than they would be using the default \texttt{span\ =\ 0.75}.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Each of these is ``straight enough'' for our purposes, in initially fitting the data.
\item
  If one of these was not, we might consider a transformation of the relevant predictor, or, if all were problematic, we might transform the outcome Y.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{residuals-vs.-predicted-values-to-check-for-non-linearity}{%
\subsection{Residuals vs.~Predicted Values to Check for Non-Linearity}\label{residuals-vs.-predicted-values-to-check-for-non-linearity}}

The residuals should appear to have no pattern (no curve, for instance) with respect to the predicted (fitted) values. It is a very good idea to plot the residuals against the fitted values to check for patterns, especially bends or other indications of non-linearity. For a multiple regression, the fitted values are a combination of the x's given by the regression equation, so they combine the effects of the x's in a way that makes sense for our particular regression model. That makes them a good choice to plot against. We'll check for other things in this plot, as well.

When you ask R to plot the result of a linear model, it will produce up to five separate plots: the first of which is a plot of \textbf{residuals vs.~fitted values}.

\hypertarget{using-plotmodel-which-1}{%
\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Using \texttt{plot(model,\ which\ =\ 1)}}{Using plot(model, which = 1)}}\label{using-plotmodel-which-1}}

To obtain this plot for the model including \texttt{sbp}, \texttt{bmi} and \texttt{dibpat} to predict \texttt{chol} we have two options. The simpler approach uses the following code to indicate plot 1 using the \texttt{which} command within the \texttt{plot} function.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(chol_m1, }\DataTypeTok{which=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-315-1} \end{center}

A smooth is again added (in red) to help you identify serious non-linearity. In this case, I would conclude that there were no serious problems with linearity in these data.

The plot also, by default, identifies the three values\footnote{If you wanted to identify more or fewer points, you could, i.e. `plot(modelname, which=1, id.n = 2)`} with the largest (in absolute value) residuals.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Here, these are rows 29, 113 and 158, each of which has a positive residual (i.e.~they represent under-predictions by the model.) To identify these points in the data, use the \texttt{slice} function.
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{slice}\NormalTok{(wcgs_}\DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{29}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{113}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{158}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 3 x 5
     id   sbp   bmi dibpat  chol
  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>  <dbl>
1  3604   124  29.4 Type A   346
2  2280   120  25.9 Type A   414
3 10360   140  23.4 Type B   349
\end{verbatim}

These turn out to be the observations with \texttt{id} = 3604, 2280 and 10360, respectively.

\hypertarget{using-ggplot2-to-plot-residuals-vs.-predicted-values}{%
\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Using \texttt{ggplot2} to plot Residuals vs.~Predicted Values}{Using ggplot2 to plot Residuals vs.~Predicted Values}}\label{using-ggplot2-to-plot-residuals-vs.-predicted-values}}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{# requires library(ggrepel)}

\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .fitted, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ .resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{               }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.resid), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{),}
             \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{               }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.resid), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{),}
               \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id), }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_abline}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{lty =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Residuals vs. Fitted Values from chol_m1"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{caption =} \StringTok{"5 largest |residuals| highlighted in red."}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Fitted Total Cholesterol"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Residual"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{aspect.ratio =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-317-1.pdf}

\hypertarget{residuals-vs.-predictors-to-further-check-for-non-linearity}{%
\subsection{Residuals vs.~Predictors To Further Check for Non-Linearity}\label{residuals-vs.-predictors-to-further-check-for-non-linearity}}

If we do see evidence of non-linearity in the plot of residuals against fitted values, I usually then proceed to look at residual plots against each of the individual predictors, in turn, to try to identify the specific predictor (or predictors) where we may need to use a transformation.

The appeal of such plots (as compared to the initial scatterplots we looked at of the outcome against each predictor) is that they eliminate the distraction of the linear fit, and let us look more closely at the non-linear part of the relationship between the outcome and each quantitative predictor. We might also look at a boxplot of the relationship between the outcome and a categorical predictor, although this is primarily for the purpose of assessing the potential for non-constant variance, rather than non-linearity.

Although I don't think you need them here, here are these plots for the residuals from our \texttt{chol\_m1} model.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ sbp, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ chol)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =} \OtherTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"lm"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{se =} \OtherTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"tomato"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{p2 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ bmi, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ chol)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =} \OtherTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"lm"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{se =} \OtherTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"tomato"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{p3 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ dibpat, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ chol)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_violin}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ dibpat)) }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{geom_boxplot}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{width =} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{coord_flip}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{guides}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{fill =} \OtherTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{(p1 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p2) }\OperatorTok{/}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p3 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{plot_layout}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{heights =} \KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{plot_annotation}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Residuals vs. Predictors in chol_m1 model"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-318-1.pdf}

Again, I see no particularly problematic issues with the assumption of linearity in either of the scatterplots here.

\hypertarget{the-independence-assumption}{%
\section{The Independence Assumption}\label{the-independence-assumption}}

The errors in the true underlying regression model must be mutually independent, but there is no way to be sure that the independence assumption is true. Fortunately, although there can be many predictor variables, there is only one outcome variable and only one set of errors. The independence assumption concerns the errors, so we check the residuals.

\textbf{Randomization condition.} The data should arise from a random sample, or from a randomized experiment.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The residuals should appear to be randomly scattered and show no patterns, trends or clumps when plotted against the predicted values.
\item
  In the special case when an x-variable is related to time, make sure that the residuals do not have a pattern when plotted against time.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{residuals-vs.-fitted-values-to-check-for-dependence}{%
\subsection{Residuals vs.~Fitted Values to Check for Dependence}\label{residuals-vs.-fitted-values-to-check-for-dependence}}

The \texttt{wcgs\_500} men were not related in any way to each other except that they were drawn from the same 10 employers, and the data are cross-sectional here, so we can be pretty sure that their measurements are independent. The residuals vs.~fitted values plots we've already seen show no clear trend, cycle or pattern which concerns us.

\hypertarget{the-constant-variance-assumption}{%
\section{The Constant Variance Assumption}\label{the-constant-variance-assumption}}

The variability of our outcome, y, should be about the same for all values of every predictor x. Of course, we can't check every combination of x values, so we look at scatterplots and check to see if the plot shows a ``fan'' shape - in essence, the \textbf{Does the plot thicken? Condition.}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Scatterplots of residuals against each x or against the predicted values, offer a visual check.
\item
  Be alert for a ``fan'' or a ``funnel'' shape showing growing/shrinking variability in one part of the plot.
\end{itemize}

Reviewing the same plots we have previously seen, I can find no evidence of substantial ``plot thickening'' in either the plot of residuals vs.~fitted values or in any of the plots of the residuals against each predictor separately.

\hypertarget{the-scale-location-plot-to-check-for-non-constant-variance}{%
\subsection{The Scale-Location Plot to Check for Non-Constant Variance}\label{the-scale-location-plot-to-check-for-non-constant-variance}}

R does provide an additional plot to help assess this issue as linear model diagnostic plot 3. This one looks at the square root of the standardized residual plotted against the fitted values. You want the loess smooth in this plot to be flat, rather than sloped.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(chol_m1, }\DataTypeTok{which=}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-319-1} \end{center}

We can replicate this plot using \texttt{ggplot2} as follows. With a \texttt{loess} smooth with \texttt{span\ =\ 2}, there's no suggestion of a meaningful trend up or down in this plot.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .fitted, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.std.resid)))) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{               }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.std.resid)), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
             \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{               }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.std.resid)), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
               \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id), }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Scale-Location Plot for chol_m1 model"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{caption =} \StringTok{"3 largest |Standardized Residual| in red."}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Fitted Value of Total Cholesterol"}\NormalTok{, }
       \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Square Root of |Standardized Residual|"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{aspect.ratio =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-320-1.pdf}

\hypertarget{what-does-trouble-look-like}{%
\subsection{What does trouble look like?}\label{what-does-trouble-look-like}}

It's helpful to see how this works in practice. Here are three sets of plots for two settings, where the left plots in each pair show simulated data that are homoscedastic (variance is constant across the levels of the predictor) and the right plots show data that are heteroscedastic (variance is not constant across predictor levels.) \emph{Source}: This example is modified from \url{http://goo.gl/weMI0U} {[}from stats.stackexchange.com{]}

Here, I'll create a tibble containing fake data on a predictor (x) and two outcomes (y1 or y2). The relationship between \texttt{y1} and \texttt{x} is meant to show no problems with the assumption of homoscedasticity. The relationship between \texttt{y2} and \texttt{x}, on the other hand, should show severe problems with this assumption.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{N  =}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{250}    \CommentTok{## original n = 500}
\NormalTok{b0 =}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{3}
\NormalTok{b1 =}\StringTok{ }\FloatTok{0.4}

\NormalTok{s2 =}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{5}
\NormalTok{g1 =}\StringTok{ }\FloatTok{1.5}     \CommentTok{## original g1 = 1.5}
\NormalTok{g2 =}\StringTok{ }\FloatTok{0.02}    \CommentTok{## original g2 = 0.015}

\NormalTok{x        =}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{runif}\NormalTok{(N, }\DataTypeTok{min=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{max=}\DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{y1   =}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{b0 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{b1}\OperatorTok{*}\NormalTok{x }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(N, }\DataTypeTok{mean=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sd=}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(s2            ))}
\NormalTok{y2 =}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{b0 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{b1}\OperatorTok{*}\NormalTok{x }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(N, }\DataTypeTok{mean=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sd=}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{exp}\NormalTok{(g1 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{g2}\OperatorTok{*}\NormalTok{x)))}

\NormalTok{fake_}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{ <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{id =} \DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{:}\DecValTok{250}\NormalTok{, x, y1, y2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

First, we'll fit our two models, and obtain augmented values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fake_m1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(y1 }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ fake_}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\CommentTok{# no problems}
\NormalTok{fake_m2 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(y2 }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ fake_}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\CommentTok{# non-constant variance}

\NormalTok{aug_fake1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{augment}\NormalTok{(fake_m1, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ fake_}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{aug_fake2 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{augment}\NormalTok{(fake_m2, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ fake_}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Now, let's look at the plots, developed first using \texttt{ggplot2}.

First, here are the two scatterplots, for our \texttt{fake\_1} model with constant variance on the left, and our \texttt{fake\_2} model with non-constant variance on the right.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p1a <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(fake_}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ x, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ y1)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"lm"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F, }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"tomato"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Constant Variance (fake_m1)"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Scatterplot of y1 vs. x"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p1b <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(fake_}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ x, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ y2)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"lm"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F, }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"tomato"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Non-Constant Variance (fake_m2)"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Scatterplot of y2 vs. x"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p1a }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p1b }
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-322-1.pdf}

Second, here are the two plots of residuals vs.~fitted values, for our \texttt{fake\_1} model with constant variance on the left, and our \texttt{fake\_2} model with non-constant variance on the right.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p2a <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_fake1, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .fitted, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ .resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_fake1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{                   }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.resid), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{),}
               \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_fake1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{                        }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.resid), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{),}
                    \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id), }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_abline}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{lty =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Constant Variance (fake_m1)"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Residuals vs. Fitted Values"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{caption =} \StringTok{"5 largest |residuals| highlighted in red."}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Fitted Value of y1"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Residual"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{aspect.ratio =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p2b <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_fake2, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .fitted, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ .resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_fake2 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{                   }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.resid), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{),}
               \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_fake2 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{                        }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.resid), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{),}
                    \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id), }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_abline}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{lty =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Non-Constant Variance (fake_m2)"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Residuals vs. Fitted Values"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{caption =} \StringTok{"5 largest |residuals| highlighted in red."}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Fitted Value of y1"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Residual"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{aspect.ratio =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p2a }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p2b }
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-323-1.pdf}

Finally, here is the scale-location plot for each scenario.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p3a <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_fake1, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .fitted, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.std.resid)))) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_fake1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{               }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.std.resid)), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
             \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_fake1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{               }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.std.resid)), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
               \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id), }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Constant Variance (fake_m1)"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Scale-Location Plot"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{caption =} \StringTok{"3 largest |Standardized Residual| in red."}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Fitted Value"}\NormalTok{, }
       \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Square Root of |Standardized Residual|"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{aspect.ratio =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p3b <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_fake2, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .fitted, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.std.resid)))) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_fake2 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{               }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.std.resid)), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
             \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_fake2 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{               }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.std.resid)), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
               \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id), }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Non-Constant Variance (fake_m2)"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Scale-Location Plot"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{caption =} \StringTok{"3 largest |Standardized Residual| in red."}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Fitted Value"}\NormalTok{, }
       \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Square Root of |Standardized Residual|"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{aspect.ratio =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p3a }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p3b}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-324-1.pdf}

Note the funnel shape for the upper two heteroscedastic (non-constant variance) plots, and the upward sloping loess line in the last one.

Here are the relevant plots for the same scenario using base R.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{par}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{mfrow=}\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{))}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(y1 }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ fake_}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{main=}\StringTok{"Constant Variance"}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(y2 }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ fake_}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{main=}\StringTok{"Non-Constant Variance"}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(fake_m1, }\DataTypeTok{which=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(fake_m2, }\DataTypeTok{which=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(fake_m1, }\DataTypeTok{which=}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(fake_m2, }\DataTypeTok{which=}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-325-1.pdf}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{par}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{mfrow=}\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\hypertarget{the-normality-assumption}{%
\section{The Normality Assumption}\label{the-normality-assumption}}

If the plot is straight enough, the data are independent, and the plots don't thicken, you can now move on to the final assumption, that of Normality.

We assume that the errors around the idealized regression model at any specified values of the x-variables follow a Normal model. We need this assumption so that we can use a Student's t-model for inference. As with other times when we've used Student's t, we'll settle for the residuals satisfying the \textbf{Nearly Normal condition}. To assess this, we simply look at a histogram or Normal probability plot of the residuals. Note that the Normality Assumption also becomes less important as the sample size grows.

\hypertarget{outlier-diagnostics-points-with-unusual-residuals}{%
\section{Outlier Diagnostics: Points with Unusual Residuals}\label{outlier-diagnostics-points-with-unusual-residuals}}

A multiple regression model will always have a point which displays the most unusual residual (that is, the residual furthest from zero in either a positive or negative direction.) As part of our assessment of the normality assumption, we will often try to decide whether the residuals follow a Normal distribution by creating a Q-Q plot of standardized residuals.

\hypertarget{standardized-residuals}{%
\subsection{Standardized Residuals}\label{standardized-residuals}}

Standardized residuals are scaled to have mean zero and a constant standard deviation of 1, as a result of dividing the original (raw) residuals by an estimate of the standard deviation that uses all of the data in the data set.

The \texttt{augment} function, when applied to a linear regression model, will generate the standardized residuals and store them in the \texttt{.std.resid} column.

If multiple regression assumptions hold, then the standardized residuals (in addition to following a Normal distribution in general) will also have mean zero and standard deviation 1, with approximately 95\% of values between -2 and +2, and approximately 99.74\% of values between -3 and +3.
