A natural check, therefore, will be to identify the most extreme/unusual standardized residual in our data set, and see whether its value is within the general bounds implied by the Empirical Rule associated with the Normal distribution. If, for instance, we see a standardized residual below -3 or above +3, this is likely to be a very poorly fit point (we would expect to see a point like this about 2.6 times for every 1,000 observations.)

A very poorly fitting point, especially if it also has high influence on the model (a concept we'll discuss shortly), may be sufficiently different from the rest of the points in the data set to merit its removal before modeling. If we did remove such a point, we'd have to have a good reason for this exclusion, and include that explanation in our report.

R's general plot set for a linear model includes (as plot 2) a Normal Q-Q plot of the standardized residuals from the model.

\hypertarget{checking-the-normality-assumption-with-a-plot}{%
\subsection{Checking the Normality Assumption with a Plot}\label{checking-the-normality-assumption-with-a-plot}}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(chol_m1, }\DataTypeTok{which=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-326-1} \end{center}

or we can use the \texttt{ggplot2} version:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{sample =}\NormalTok{ .std.resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_qq}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_qq_line}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Normal Q-Q plot"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Standardized Residual from chol_m1"}\NormalTok{, }
       \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Standard Normal Quantiles"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{aspect.ratio =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p2 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ .std.resid, }\DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{""}\NormalTok{)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_violin}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"dodgerblue"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_boxplot}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{width =} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Box and Violin Plot"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Standardized Residual from chol_m1"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"mod_1"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p1 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p2 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{plot_layout}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{widths =} \KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{plot_annotation}\NormalTok{(}
    \DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Normality of Standardized Residuals from mod_1"}\NormalTok{,}
    \DataTypeTok{caption =} \KeywordTok{paste0}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"n = "}\NormalTok{, }
                     \KeywordTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(.std.resid)),}
                     \StringTok{" residual values are plotted here."}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-327-1.pdf}

The Q-Q plot of standardized residuals shows one point (in row 113, according to the plot) that is far away from our expectations under the Normal model. Let's look at that point a little more closely.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{slice}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{113}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id}\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{.std.resid) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{dig =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tabular}{r|r|r|l|r|r|r|r}
\hline
id & sbp & bmi & dibpat & chol & .fitted & .resid & .std.resid\\
\hline
2280 & 120 & 25.86 & Type A & 414 & 227.61 & 186.39 & 4.71\\
\hline
\end{tabular}

The standardized residual here is 4.71. That seems excessively large, in a sample of 250 observations.

\hypertarget{assessing-standardized-residuals-with-an-outlier-test}{%
\subsection{Assessing Standardized Residuals with an Outlier Test}\label{assessing-standardized-residuals-with-an-outlier-test}}

Is a standardized residual of 4.71 particularly unusual in a sample of 250 such standardized residuals, given that we are trying to assume that these are well modeled by a Normal distribution, supposedly with mean 0 and standard deviation 1?

Yes. The \texttt{car} library has a test called \texttt{outlierTest} which can be applied to a linear model to see if the most unusual studentized residual in absolute value is a surprise given the sample size (the studentized residual is very similar to the standardized residual - it simply uses a different estimate for the standard deviation for every point, in each case excluding the point it is currently studying)

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{# requires library(car)}

\KeywordTok{outlierTest}\NormalTok{(chol_m1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
    rstudent unadjusted p-value Bonferroni p
113 4.816993         1.9404e-06   0.00097019
\end{verbatim}

Our conclusion from the Bonferroni p value here is that there's evidence to support the belief that we could have a serious problem with Normality, in that a studentized residual of 4.82 is out of the range we might reasonably expect to see given a Normal distribution of errors and 250 observations.

Which value is that? It's the subject with \texttt{id} = 2280.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{slice}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{113}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id}\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{.std.resid) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{dig =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tabular}{r|r|r|l|r|r|r|r}
\hline
id & sbp & bmi & dibpat & chol & .fitted & .resid & .std.resid\\
\hline
2280 & 120 & 25.86 & Type A & 414 & 227.61 & 186.39 & 4.71\\
\hline
\end{tabular}

In terms of solutions, we could consider dropping the point, but this would leave us with the vexing problem of how, exactly, to justify that choice. One question we might ask is whether dropping that point (row 113, or \texttt{id} = 2280) from our \texttt{chol\_m1} model have a big impact?

\hypertarget{outlier-diagnostics-identifying-points-with-unusually-high-leverage}{%
\section{Outlier Diagnostics: Identifying Points with Unusually High Leverage}\label{outlier-diagnostics-identifying-points-with-unusually-high-leverage}}

An observation can be an outlier not just in terms of having an unusual residual, but also in terms of having an unusual combination of predictor values. Such an observation is described as having high leverage in a data set.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The \texttt{augment} function stores the leverage for each point in the \texttt{.hat} variable.
\item
  The average leverage value is equal to k/n, where n is the number of observations included in the regression model, and k is the number of coefficients (slopes + intercept).
\item
  Any point with a leverage value greater than 3 times the average leverage of a point in the data set is one that should be investigated closely.
\end{itemize}

For instance, in the \texttt{wcgs\_500} data, we have 500 observations, so the average leverage will be 4/500 = 0.008 and a high leverage point would have leverage \(\geq\) 12/500 or .024. We can check this out directly, with a sorted list of leverage values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id}\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{chol, .hat) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(.hat }\OperatorTok{>}\StringTok{ }\FloatTok{0.024}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{arrange}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{desc}\NormalTok{(.hat))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 12 x 6
      id   sbp   bmi dibpat  chol   .hat
   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>  <dbl>  <dbl>
 1 19078   230  24.4 Type B   254 0.0884
 2 10214   170  37.7 Type B   153 0.0600
 3 22025   210  26.5 Type B   187 0.0548
 4 10075   150  35.3 Type B   224 0.0401
 5 16031   200  28.1 Type A   256 0.0392
 6 11432   196  27.3 Type B   178 0.0384
 7  3085   190  22.3 Type A   241 0.0383
 8 13389   120  33.0 Type A   232 0.0303
 9 12976   106  15.7 Type B   207 0.0287
10  3182   120  32.3 Type A   218 0.0262
11 19099   104  30.5 Type A   234 0.0252
12  3235   184  26.6 Type A   274 0.0248
\end{verbatim}

Twelve of our 500 subjects meet the standard for large leverage values (\texttt{.hat} values exceeding three times the average \texttt{.hat} value) but none of these are the outlier (\texttt{id} = 2280) we identified as having an especially poor fit (large standardized residual.)

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(id }\OperatorTok{==}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{2280}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id, .std.resid, .hat)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 1 x 3
     id .std.resid    .hat
  <dbl>      <dbl>   <dbl>
1  2280       4.71 0.00555
\end{verbatim}

Another way to understand the impact of leverage is to look at a plot of residuals vs.~leverage, which is diagnostic plot 5 for a linear model. Here's the base R version for our \texttt{chol\_m1} model.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(chol_m1, }\DataTypeTok{which=}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-333-1} \end{center}

Here is the \texttt{ggplot2} version.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .hat, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ .std.resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(.cooksd }\OperatorTok{>=}\StringTok{ }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{),}
             \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(.cooksd }\OperatorTok{>=}\StringTok{ }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{),}
               \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id), }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_vline}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{xintercept =} \DecValTok{3}\OperatorTok{*}\KeywordTok{mean}\NormalTok{(.hat)), }\DataTypeTok{lty =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Residuals vs. Leverage"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Leverage"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Standardized Residual"}\NormalTok{) }
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-334-1.pdf}

We see that the most highly leveraged points are shown furthest to the right in this plot (in fact, I've inserted a dotted vertical line at the cutpoint of \texttt{3*mean(.hat)}), but our problematic standardized residual has a very typical leverage value (at the top left of this plot.)

Let's look at our highest leveraged point (\texttt{id} = 19078 from our list above) and remember that this just means that this point is unusual in terms of its predictor values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(id }\OperatorTok{==}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{19078}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id, sbp, bmi, dibpat, .hat)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 1 x 5
     id   sbp   bmi dibpat   .hat
  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>   <dbl>
1 19078   230  24.4 Type B 0.0884
\end{verbatim}

Compare these findings to what we see in the \texttt{wcgs\_500} data overall. Let's focus on the quantitative variables involved in our model, \texttt{sbp} and \texttt{bmi}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{# requires library(mosaic)}

\KeywordTok{df_stats}\NormalTok{(}\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{bmi, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_}\DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
  response      min        Q1   median       Q3       max      mean        sd
1      sbp 100.0000 120.00000 126.0000 140.0000 230.00000 129.39800 16.548453
2      bmi  15.6605  23.03123  24.5412  26.1554  37.65281  24.65342  2.546486
    n missing
1 500       0
2 500       0
\end{verbatim}

Note that our highly leveraged point has the largest \texttt{sbp} of all 500 subjects, although a rather typical \texttt{bmi}.

If you're wondering about the rest of our \texttt{chol\_m1} model, there are only two possible values of \texttt{dibpat} and so a point cannot really be an outlier on that variable.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{wcgs_}\DecValTok{500} \OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{tabyl}\NormalTok{(dibpat)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
 dibpat   n percent
 Type A 259   0.518
 Type B 241   0.482
\end{verbatim}

Remember that 12 of the points in our \texttt{chol\_m1} model showed up with leverage values more than three times higher than the mean leverage.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(.hat }\OperatorTok{>}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{3} \OperatorTok{*}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{mean}\NormalTok{(.hat)) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{nrow}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] 12
\end{verbatim}

So, let's look at a plot of the two quantitative predictors in our data to see which 12 points show up as those with ``high'' leverage.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ sbp, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ bmi)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"gray50"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{                   }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(.hat, }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{),}
               \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Systolic BP"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Body Mass Index"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Where are the highly leveraged points in our model?"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"wcgs_500 data, model chol_1, 12 highest leverage points shown in red"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-339-1.pdf}

The points with high leverage (large values of \texttt{.hat}) are simply those with an unusual combination of the two quantitative predictors.

\hypertarget{outlier-diagnostics-identifying-points-with-high-influence-on-the-model}{%
\section{Outlier Diagnostics: Identifying Points with High Influence on the Model}\label{outlier-diagnostics-identifying-points-with-high-influence-on-the-model}}

A point in a regression model has high \textbf{influence} if the inclusion (or exclusion) of that point will have substantial impact on the model, in terms of the estimated coefficients, and the summaries of fit. The measure I routinely use to describe the level of influence a point has on the model is called \textbf{Cook's distance}.

As a general rule, a Cook's distance greater than 1 indicates a point likely to have substantial influence on the model, while a point in the 0.5 to 1.0 range is only occasionally worthy of investigation. Observations with Cook's distance below 0.5 are unlikely to influence the model in any substantial way.

\hypertarget{assessing-the-value-of-cooks-distance}{%
\subsection{Assessing the Value of Cook's Distance}\label{assessing-the-value-of-cooks-distance}}

You can obtain information on Cook's distance in several ways in R.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  My favorite is model diagnostic plot 5 (the residuals vs.~leverage plot) which we've seen before and which I repeat below. This plot uses red contours to indicate the value of Cook's distance.

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    This is possible because influence, as measured by Cook's distance, is a function of both the observation's standardized residual and its leverage.
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(chol_m1, }\DataTypeTok{which=}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-340-1} \end{center}

No points in this plot indicate substantial influence on our model. To do so, they'd have to be outside the Cook's distance contour shown at the top right of this plot (there is a similar arc at the bottom right, but it's too far away from the data to show up in the plot.)

\hypertarget{index-plot-of-cooks-distance}{%
\subsection{Index Plot of Cook's Distance}\label{index-plot-of-cooks-distance}}

Model Diagnostic Plot 4 for a linear model is an index plot of Cook's distance.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(chol_m1, }\DataTypeTok{which=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-341-1} \end{center}

Or, if you like, run the \texttt{ggplot2} version:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{aug_chol1_ex <-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{obsnum =} \DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{:}\KeywordTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(.cooksd)))}

\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_chol1_ex, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ obsnum, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ .cooksd)) }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_segment}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ obsnum, }\DataTypeTok{xend =}\NormalTok{ obsnum, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{yend =}\NormalTok{ .cooksd)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_chol1_ex }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{               }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(.cooksd, }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
               \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Row Number"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Cook's Distance"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Cook's distance Index plot for chol_m1"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Subjects with the 3 largest Cook's d values are identified."}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-342-1.pdf}

It is clear from this plot that the largest Cook's distance (somewhere between 0.05 and 0.06) is for the observation in subject 10214 (or row 131 of the data set, depending on which version you build. All of the Cook's distance values are stored in the \texttt{.cooksd} variable in our augmented results. Let's look at the six largest Cook's distance values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id, .std.resid, .hat, .cooksd) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{arrange}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{desc}\NormalTok{(.cooksd)) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{head}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{dig =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tabular}{r|r|r|r}
\hline
id & .std.resid & .hat & .cooksd\\
\hline
10214 & -1.779 & 0.060 & 0.051\\
\hline
2280 & 4.713 & 0.006 & 0.031\\
\hline
3604 & 3.033 & 0.012 & 0.029\\
\hline
22025 & -1.308 & 0.055 & 0.025\\
\hline
11432 & -1.432 & 0.038 & 0.020\\
\hline
12392 & -2.661 & 0.010 & 0.017\\
\hline
\end{tabular}

Remember that we'd need a Cook's distance value to be at least 0.50 for us to worry about it in a serious way. Here, the largest we see (0.05 for \texttt{id} = 10214) is still far away from that standard.

What is the story on point 10214? Why is it unusual?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{aug_chol1 }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(id }\OperatorTok{==}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{10214}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id}\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{.fitted, .std.resid, .hat, .cooksd) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{dig =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tabular}{r|r|r|l|r|r|r|r|r}
\hline
id & sbp & bmi & dibpat & chol & .fitted & .std.resid & .hat & .cooksd\\
\hline
10214 & 170 & 37.65 & Type B & 153 & 221.42 & -1.78 & 0.06 & 0.05\\
\hline
\end{tabular}

This turns out to be the subject with the largest BMI in the data, hence some leverage, and a somewhat large (-1.78) but not enormous standardized residual. Hence the combination suggests some influence on the model.

\hypertarget{running-a-regression-model-while-excluding-a-point}{%
\section{Running a Regression Model While Excluding A Point}\label{running-a-regression-model-while-excluding-a-point}}

Suppose that we wanted to remove the point with the most influence over the model. We could fit a model to the data without \texttt{id} = 10214 to see the impact of this change. Let's try it.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{summary}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(chol }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{bmi }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dibpat, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_}\DecValTok{500} \OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(id }\OperatorTok{!=}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{10214}\NormalTok{)))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

Call:
lm(formula = chol ~ sbp + bmi + dibpat, data = wcgs_500 %>% filter(id != 
    10214))

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-113.062  -25.453   -2.469   23.954  186.200 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  206.3207    20.3647  10.131  < 2e-16 ***
sbp            0.2464     0.1124   2.192  0.02883 *  
bmi           -0.3127     0.7438  -0.420  0.67433    
dibpatType B -10.6424     3.5635  -2.986  0.00296 ** 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 39.57 on 495 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.02943,	Adjusted R-squared:  0.02355 
F-statistic: 5.003 on 3 and 495 DF,  p-value: 0.001996
\end{verbatim}

Compare these results to those obtained with the full data set, shown below.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  How is the model affected by removing subject 10214?
\item
  What is the impact on the slopes?
\item
  On the summary measures? Residuals?
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{summary}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(chol }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{bmi }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dibpat, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_}\DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

Call:
lm(formula = chol ~ sbp + bmi + dibpat, data = wcgs_500)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-113.133  -25.689   -1.932   24.036  186.390 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  214.5185    19.8825  10.789  < 2e-16 ***
sbp            0.2361     0.1125   2.099  0.03634 *  
bmi           -0.5894     0.7290  -0.808  0.41921    
dibpatType B -11.0408     3.5643  -3.098  0.00206 ** 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 39.66 on 496 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.02954,	Adjusted R-squared:  0.02367 
F-statistic: 5.033 on 3 and 496 DF,  p-value: 0.001915
\end{verbatim}

While it is true that we can sometimes improve the performance of the model in some ways by removing this point, there's no good reason to do so. We can't just remove a point from the data set without a good reason (and, to be clear, ``I ran my model and it doesn't fit this point well'' is NOT a good reason). Good reasons would include:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  This observation was included in the sample in error, for instance, because the subject was not eligible.
\item
  An error was made in transcribing the value of this observation to the final data set.
\item
  And, sometimes, even ``This observation is part of a meaningful subgroup of patients that I had always intended to model separately\ldots{}'' assuming that's true.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{summarizing-regression-diagnostics-for-431}{%
\section{Summarizing Regression Diagnostics for 431}\label{summarizing-regression-diagnostics-for-431}}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Check the ``straight enough'' condition with scatterplots of the y variable (outcome) against each x-variable (predictor), usually via the top row of a scatterplot matrix.
\item
  If the data are straight enough (that is, if it looks like the regression model is plausible), fit a regression model, to obtain residuals and influence measures.

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    If not, consider using the Box-Cox approach to identify a possible transformation for the outcome variable, and then recheck the straight enough condition.
  \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{The \texttt{plot} function for a fitted linear model builds five diagnostic plots.}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{2}
\item
  {[}\textbf{Plot 1}{]} A scatterplot of the residuals against the fitted values.

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    This plot should look patternless. Check in particular for any bend (which would suggest that the data weren't all that straight after all) and for any thickening, which would indicate non-constant variance.
  \item
    If the data are measured over time, check especially for evidence of patterns that might suggest they are not independent. For example, plot the residuals against time to look for patterns.
  \item
    These values are stored as \texttt{.resid} and \texttt{.fitted} by the \texttt{augment()} function applied to a linear model.
  \end{itemize}
\item
  {[}\textbf{Plot 3}{]} A scale-location plot of the square root of the standardized residuals against the fitted values to look for a non-flat loess smooth, which indicates non-constant variance. Standardized residuals are obtained via \texttt{augment} and stored in the \texttt{.std.resid} variable.
\item
  {[}\textbf{Plot 2}{]} If the plots above look OK, then consider a Normal Q-Q plot of the standardized residuals to check the nearly Normal condition.
\item
  {[}\textbf{Plot 5}{]} The final plot we often look at is the plot of residuals vs.~leverage, with influence contours. Sometimes, we'll also look at {[}\textbf{Plot 4}{]} the index plot of the Cook's distance for each observation in the data set.

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    To look for points with substantial \textbf{leverage} on the model by virtue of having unusual values of the predictors - look for points whose leverage is at least 3 times as large as the average leverage value.
    - The average leverage is always k/n, where k is the number of coefficients fit by the model (including the slopes and intercept), and n is the number of observations in the model.
    - To obtain the leverage values, use the \texttt{augment()} function which stores them in \texttt{.hat}.
  \item
    To look for points with substantial \textbf{influence} on the model, that is, removing them from the model would change it substantially, consider the Cook's distance, plotted in contours in Plot 5, or in an index plot in Plot 4.
    - Any Cook's d \textgreater{} 1 will likely have a substantial impact on the model.
    - Even points with Cook's d \textgreater{} 0.5 may merit further investigation.
    - Find the Cook's distances using the \texttt{augment()} function, which stores them in \texttt{.cooksd}.
  \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\hypertarget{violated-assumptions-problematic-residual-plots}{%
\section{Violated Assumptions: Problematic Residual Plots?}\label{violated-assumptions-problematic-residual-plots}}

So what do serious assumption violations look like, and what can we do about them?

\hypertarget{problems-with-linearity}{%
\section{Problems with Linearity}\label{problems-with-linearity}}

Here is a simulated example that shows a clear problem with non-linearity.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{4311}\NormalTok{); x1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{mean =} \DecValTok{15}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sd =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{4312}\NormalTok{); x2 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{mean =} \DecValTok{10}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sd =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{4313}\NormalTok{); e1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{mean =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sd =} \DecValTok{15}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{y <-}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{15} \OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x1 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x2}\OperatorTok{^}\DecValTok{2} \OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{e1}
\NormalTok{viol1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{data.frame}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{outcome =}\NormalTok{ y, }\DataTypeTok{pred1 =}\NormalTok{ x1, }\DataTypeTok{pred2 =}\NormalTok{ x2) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{tbl_df}
\NormalTok{model}\FloatTok{.1}\NormalTok{ <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(outcome }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{pred1 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{pred2, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ viol1)}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.1}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{which =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/violsim 1-1.pdf}

In light of this, I would be looking for a potential transformation of outcome. Does the Box-Cox plot make any useful suggestions?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{boxCox}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.1}\NormalTok{); }\KeywordTok{powerTransform}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.1}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/boxCox_1_violsim1-1.pdf}

\begin{verbatim}
Estimated transformation parameter 
       Y1 
0.4414775 
\end{verbatim}

Note that if the outcome was negative, we would have to add some constant value to every outcome in order to get every outcome value to be positive, and Box-Cox to run. This suggests fitting a new model, using the square root of the outcome.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{model}\FloatTok{.2}\NormalTok{ <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(outcome) }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{pred1 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{pred2, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ viol1)}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{which =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/violsim_2-1.pdf}

This is meaningfully better in terms of curve, but now looks a bit fan-shaped, indicating a potential problem with heteroscedasticity. Let's look at the scale-location plot for this model.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{which =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/violsim_2b-1.pdf}

This definitely looks like there's a trend down in this plot. So the square root transformation, by itself, probably hasn't resolved assumptions sufficiently well. We'll have to be very careful about our interpretation of the model.

\hypertarget{problems-with-non-normality-an-influential-point}{%
\section{Problems with Non-Normality: An Influential Point}\label{problems-with-non-normality-an-influential-point}}

With 100 observations, a single value with a standardized residual above 3 is very surprising. In our initial model.1 here, we have a standardized residual value as large as 6, so we clearly have a problem with that outlier.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.1}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{which =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/violsim_2c-1.pdf}

Should we, perhaps, remove point 72, and try again? Only if we have a reason beyond ``it was poorly fit'' to drop that point. Is point 72 highly leveraged or influential?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.1}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{which =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/violsim_5-1.pdf}

What if we drop this point (72) and fit our linear model again. Does this resolve our difficulty with the assumption of linearity?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{model.}\FloatTok{1.}\NormalTok{no72 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(outcome }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{pred1 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{pred2, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ viol1[}\OperatorTok{-}\DecValTok{72}\NormalTok{,])}
\KeywordTok{par}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{mfrow=}\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{))}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.1}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{which =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{caption =} \StringTok{"With Point 72"}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(model.}\FloatTok{1.}\NormalTok{no72, }\DataTypeTok{which =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{caption =} \StringTok{"Without Point 72"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/viol_fit_without_72-1.pdf}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{par}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{mfrow=}\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

No, it doesn't. But what if we combine our outcome transformation with dropping point 72?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{model.}\FloatTok{2.}\NormalTok{no72 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(outcome) }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{pred1 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{pred2, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ viol1[}\OperatorTok{-}\DecValTok{72}\NormalTok{,])}
\KeywordTok{par}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{mfrow=}\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{))}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(model.}\FloatTok{2.}\NormalTok{no72, }\DataTypeTok{which =} \KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-347-1.pdf}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{par}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{mfrow=}\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Nope. That still doesn't alleviate the problem of heteroscedasticity very well. At least, we no longer have any especially influential points, nor do we have substantial non-Normality.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{par}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{mfrow=}\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{))}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(model.}\FloatTok{2.}\NormalTok{no72, }\DataTypeTok{which =} \KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-348-1.pdf}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{par}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{mfrow=}\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

At this point, I would be considering potential transformations of the predictors, quite possibly fitting some sort of polynomial term or cubic spline term in the predictors, but I'll leave that for discussion in 432.

\hypertarget{problems-with-non-normality-skew}{%
\section{Problems with Non-Normality: Skew}\label{problems-with-non-normality-skew}}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{4314}\NormalTok{); x1 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{mean =} \DecValTok{15}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sd =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{4315}\NormalTok{); x2 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{mean =} \DecValTok{10}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sd =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{)}
\KeywordTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{4316}\NormalTok{); e2 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{mean =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{sd =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{y2 <-}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{50} \OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x1 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x2 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{e2}\OperatorTok{^}\DecValTok{2}
\NormalTok{viol2 <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{data.frame}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{outcome =}\NormalTok{ y2, }\DataTypeTok{pred1 =}\NormalTok{ x1, }\DataTypeTok{pred2 =}\NormalTok{ x2) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{tbl_df}
\NormalTok{model}\FloatTok{.3}\NormalTok{ <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(outcome }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{pred1 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{pred2, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ viol2)}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.3}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{which =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-349-1.pdf}

Skewed residuals often show up in strange patterns in the plot of residuals vs.~fitted values, too, as in this case.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.3}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{which =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-350-1.pdf}

Clearly, we have some larger residuals on the positive side, but not on the negative side. Would an outcome transformation be suggested by Box-Cox?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{boxCox}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.3}\NormalTok{); }\KeywordTok{powerTransform}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-351-1.pdf}

\begin{verbatim}
Estimated transformation parameter 
       Y1 
-1.438747 
\end{verbatim}

The suggested transformation looks like the inverse of our outcome.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{model}\FloatTok{.4}\NormalTok{ <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{/}\NormalTok{outcome }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{pred1 }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{pred2, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ viol2)}
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.4}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{which =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-352-1.pdf}

OK. That's something of an improvement. How about the other residual plots with this transformation?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{plot}\NormalTok{(model}\FloatTok{.4}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{which =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-353-1.pdf}

The impact of the skew is reduced, at least. I might well be satisfied enough with this, in practice.

\hypertarget{building-prediction-models-for-wcgs}{%
\chapter{\texorpdfstring{Building Prediction Models for \texttt{wcgs}}{Building Prediction Models for wcgs}}\label{building-prediction-models-for-wcgs}}

Sometimes, we use a regression model for description of a multivariate relationship which requires only that we provide an adequate fit to the data at hand. Should we want to use the model for prediction, then a more appropriate standard which requires us to fit a model that does each of the following three things:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  {[}fits well{]} Provides an adequate fit to the data at hand
\item
  {[}parsimonious{]} Includes only those predictors which actually have detectable predictive value
\item
  {[}predicts well out of sample{]} Does a better job predicting our outcome in new data than other alternative models
\end{enumerate}

We'll spend considerable time in 432 studying how best to validate a regression model, but for now, we'll focus on a pair of issues:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\alph{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  Given a set of predictors, how should we let the computer help us understand which subsets of those predictors are most worthy of our additional attention?
\item
  Given a pair of models to compare, what tools should we use to determine which one better predicts new data?
\end{enumerate}

\hypertarget{predicting-cholesterol-level-in-wcgs-again}{%
\section{Predicting Cholesterol Level in WCGS, Again}\label{predicting-cholesterol-level-in-wcgs-again}}

To address these issues, I'll again look at the \texttt{wcgs} data (Western Collaborative Group Study), described in Chapter \ref{WCGS-Study}, then again in Chapter \ref{Reg-Diag}.

This time, we'll try to predict the variable \texttt{chol} on the basis of some subset of the following six predictors: \texttt{age}, \texttt{bmi}, \texttt{sbp}, \texttt{dbp}, \texttt{smoke} and \texttt{dibpat}.

The steps we'll take are as follows.

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  Check the \texttt{wcgs} data for missing or out-of-range values in the variables under study, so we don't regret it later. Make a decision about how to handle issues here.
\item
  Partition the \texttt{wcgs} data into a training (development) sample of 2000 observations, and a test (holdout) sample of the remaining observations.
\item
  Using only the model development sample, fit three candidate models.

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    Model A will predict \texttt{chol} using all six predictors.
  \item
    Model B will predict \texttt{chol} using five predictors, specifically \texttt{age}, \texttt{bmi}, \texttt{dbp}, \texttt{dibpat} and \texttt{smoke}.
  \item
    Model C will predict \texttt{chol} using only three predictors, specifically \texttt{age}, \texttt{dbp} and \texttt{smoke}
  \end{itemize}
\item
  Compare the fit quality of these models in the development sample to see if any of them is superior in terms of in-sample predictive quality.
\item
  Assess regression assumptions in each of these models in the development sample.
\item
  Finally moving to the holdout sample, compare the quality of predictions made by the models in terms of several criteria to see if any of the models (A, B or C) is clearly superior in terms of out-of-sample prediction.
\end{enumerate}

\hypertarget{checking-for-missing-or-problematic-values}{%
\section{Checking for Missing or Problematic Values}\label{checking-for-missing-or-problematic-values}}

Suppose that after consulting with clinical experts, we want to ensure that:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  all \texttt{age} values are between 39 and 59 years
\item
  all \texttt{bmi} are between 15 and 50
\item
  all \texttt{sbp} are between 80 and 250 mm Hg
\item
  all \texttt{dbp} are between 50 and 200 mm Hg
\item
  all values of \texttt{sbp-dbp} are at least 10 and no more than 90 mm Hg
\item
  all values of \texttt{chol} are between 100 and 400 mg/dl
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{Hmisc}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{describe}\NormalTok{(wcgs }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(age, bmi, sbp, dbp, smoke, dibpat, chol))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
wcgs %>% select(age, bmi, sbp, dbp, smoke, dibpat, chol) 

 7  Variables      3154  Observations
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
age 
       n  missing distinct     Info     Mean      Gmd      .05      .10 
    3154        0       21    0.996    46.28    6.256       39       40 
     .25      .50      .75      .90      .95 
      42       45       50       55       57 

lowest : 39 40 41 42 43, highest: 55 56 57 58 59
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
bmi 
       n  missing distinct     Info     Mean      Gmd      .05      .10 
    3154        0      679        1    24.52    2.803    20.59    21.52 
     .25      .50      .75      .90      .95 
   22.96    24.39    25.84    27.45    28.73 

lowest : 11.19061 15.66050 16.87200 17.21633 17.22242
highest: 36.04248 37.22973 37.24805 37.65281 38.94737
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
sbp 
       n  missing distinct     Info     Mean      Gmd      .05      .10 
    3154        0       62    0.996    128.6    16.25      110      112 
     .25      .50      .75      .90      .95 
     120      126      136      148      156 

lowest :  98 100 102 104 106, highest: 200 208 210 212 230
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
dbp 
       n  missing distinct     Info     Mean      Gmd      .05      .10 
    3154        0       42    0.992    82.02    10.51       68       70 
     .25      .50      .75      .90      .95 
      76       80       86       94      100 

lowest :  58  60  62  64  66, highest: 125 129 130 136 150
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
smoke 
       n  missing distinct 
    3154        0        2 
                      
Value         No   Yes
Frequency   1652  1502
Proportion 0.524 0.476
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
dibpat 
       n  missing distinct 
    3154        0        2 
                        
Value      Type A Type B
Frequency    1589   1565
Proportion  0.504  0.496
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
chol 
       n  missing distinct     Info     Mean      Gmd      .05      .10 
    3142       12      237        1    226.4    47.99    161.1    175.0 
     .25      .50      .75      .90      .95 
   197.2    223.0    253.0    280.0    302.0 

lowest : 103 110 111 112 113, highest: 386 390 400 414 645
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
\end{verbatim}

Here are the issues I see:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The (\texttt{chol}) total cholesterol levels include 12 missing values (so we need to decide what to do about them) and values of 414 and 645 that I don't think are plausible. Since \texttt{chol} is our outcome, I'm inclined to delete those 14 subjects from our analyses.
\item
  The \texttt{bmi} value of 11.9 is much smaller than all other values, and seems implausible, so I'm inclined to delete that subject, as well.
\item
  All of the \texttt{age} values are between 39 and 59, as they should be.
\item
  The \texttt{dibpat} and \texttt{smoke} binary variables each appear to be reasonable given the timing of the data collection (the early 1960s).
\item
  The blood pressures, individually, for \texttt{sbp} and \texttt{dbp} appear reasonable in terms of their ranges and have no missing data. It's worth it to check to see that everyone has a \texttt{sbp} meaningfully larger than their \texttt{dbp}.
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{wcgs }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{bp_diff =}\NormalTok{ sbp }\OperatorTok{-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dbp) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id, sbp, dbp, bp_diff) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{slice_min}\NormalTok{(bp_diff, }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 3 x 4
     id   sbp   dbp bp_diff
  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>
1  3822   120   100      20
2 11406   104    80      24
3 10204   110    86      24
\end{verbatim}

This looks fine. Are any combinations out of line in the other direction, with a difference of 90 or more?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{wcgs }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{bp_diff =}\NormalTok{ sbp }\OperatorTok{-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dbp) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id, sbp, dbp, bp_diff) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(bp_diff }\OperatorTok{>=}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{90}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 8 x 4
     id   sbp   dbp bp_diff
  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>
1  2172   208   102     106
2 12453   196    86     110
3 12280   190    98      92
4 13268   170    80      90
5  3546   188    90      98
6  2078   188    86     102
7 19078   230   118     112
8 22025   210   108     102
\end{verbatim}

This \texttt{sbp\ -\ dbp} value is called the pulse pressure. Given that these men did not (at enrollment) have heart disease, it is surprising to see so many values separated by more than what would be typical (about 40-60 mm Hg.)

I'll be a little aggressive in my cleaning and also drop these 8 subjects with \texttt{sbp\ -\ dbp} of 90 or more mm Hg. So, we need to deal with the missingness in \texttt{chol}, the strange \texttt{bmi} value and the strange \texttt{chol} value in addition to dropping subjects with \texttt{sbp\ -\ dbp} to get to a ``clean'' data set here.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{wcgs_ms <-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{wcgs }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id, age, bmi, sbp, dbp, smoke, dibpat, chol) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{complete.cases}\NormalTok{(.)) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\CommentTok{# drops 12 subjects}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(bmi }\OperatorTok{>}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{15}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\CommentTok{# drops 1 subject}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(sbp }\OperatorTok{-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dbp }\OperatorTok{<}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{90}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\CommentTok{# drops 7 more subjects}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(chol }\OperatorTok{<=}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{400}\NormalTok{) }\CommentTok{# drops 2 more subjects}

\KeywordTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(wcgs); }\KeywordTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(wcgs_ms)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] 3154
\end{verbatim}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] 3132
\end{verbatim}

So we have dropped a total of 22 subjects from the data set. We could certainly consider imputing missing values of \texttt{chol}, but I am uncomfortable doing that for an outcome.

\hypertarget{partitioning-the-wcgsms-sample}{%
\section{\texorpdfstring{Partitioning the \texttt{wcgsms} sample}{Partitioning the wcgsms sample}}\label{partitioning-the-wcgsms-sample}}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{1}
\tightlist
\item
  Partition the \texttt{wcgs} data into a training (development) sample of 2000 observations, and a test (holdout) sample of the remaining observations.
\end{enumerate}

Before we partition, it's always a good idea to ensure that each of the subject identifiers (here, \texttt{id}) are unique.

We could simply count the number of rows in the data set and make sure it matches the number of distinct \texttt{id} values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(wcgs_ms), }\KeywordTok{n_distinct}\NormalTok{(wcgs_ms }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id)))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] 3132 3132
\end{verbatim}

Looks good. If you preferm, check directly with the \texttt{identical()} function, which should return TRUE.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{identical}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(wcgs_ms), }\KeywordTok{n_distinct}\NormalTok{(wcgs_ms }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id)))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] TRUE
\end{verbatim}

Remember to set a seed so that you can replicate the selection.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{431}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{wcgs_ms_train <-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{wcgs_ms }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{slice_sample}\NormalTok{(., }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{2000}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{wcgs_ms_test <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{anti_join}\NormalTok{(wcgs_ms, wcgs_ms_train, }\DataTypeTok{by =} \StringTok{"id"}\NormalTok{)}

\KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(wcgs_ms), }\KeywordTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(wcgs_ms_train), }\KeywordTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(wcgs_ms_test))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] 3132 2000 1132
\end{verbatim}

OK. This looks good. We have 2000 observations in the training sample, and the rest in the test sample.

Given a large sample size (at least 500 observations in the full data set) I would usually think about holding out somewhere between 20\% and 30\% of the data in this manner, but it's OK to stray a bit from those bounds, as we have here, with 1132/3132 = 36\% held out for the test sample.

\hypertarget{should-we-transform-our-outcome}{%
\section{Should we transform our outcome?}\label{should-we-transform-our-outcome}}

Consider the Box-Cox approach, which we'll check in our complete (model C) model.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{## requires library(car)}

\KeywordTok{boxCox}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(chol }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{age }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{bmi }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{smoke }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dibpat, }
             \DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_ms_train))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-361-1.pdf}

It looks like we might want to consider using the square root of the cholesterol level as our outcome. We'll try that.

\hypertarget{scatterplot-matrix-and-assessment-of-collinearity}{%
\section{Scatterplot Matrix and Assessment of Collinearity}\label{scatterplot-matrix-and-assessment-of-collinearity}}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{## requires library(GGally)}

\NormalTok{wcgs_ms_train <-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{wcgs_ms_train }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{sqrtchol =} \KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(chol))}

\KeywordTok{ggpairs}\NormalTok{(wcgs_ms_train }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(age, bmi, sbp, dbp, smoke, dibpat, sqrtchol),}
        \DataTypeTok{lower =} \KeywordTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{combo =} \KeywordTok{wrap}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"facethist"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{binwidth =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)), }
        \DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"2000 subjects in `wcgs_ms_train`"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-362-1.pdf}

With so many observations and more than just a few predictors, it's not always easy to parse this plot. We might have considered splitting it into two parts.

Let's check for collinearity. Unsurprisingly, \texttt{sbp} and \texttt{dbp} are highly correlated with each other. Does this give us problems with variance inflation? Again, we'll consider the largest model we'll fit.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{car}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{vif}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(chol }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{age }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{bmi }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{smoke }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dibpat, }
             \DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_ms_train))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
     age      bmi      sbp      dbp    smoke   dibpat 
1.038393 1.157091 2.536938 2.578828 1.035868 1.016123 
\end{verbatim}

The VIF is largest for \texttt{sbp} and \texttt{dbp}, suggesting that perhaps we won't need them both. Since we have candidate models using only \texttt{dbp}, let's see how they fare.

\hypertarget{fit-our-three-candidate-models}{%
\section{Fit our Three Candidate Models}\label{fit-our-three-candidate-models}}

Due to our choice to transform our outcome, we will now fit the following candidate models:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Model A will predict \texttt{sqrt(chol)} using all six predictors.
\item
  Model B will predict \texttt{sqrt(chol)} using five predictors, specifically \texttt{age}, \texttt{bmi}, \texttt{dbp}, \texttt{dibpat} and \texttt{smoke}.
\item
  Model C will predict \texttt{sqrt(chol)} using only two predictors, specifically \texttt{dbp} and \texttt{smoke}
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{three-candidate-models}{%
\subsection{Three Candidate Models}\label{three-candidate-models}}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{modelA <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(chol) }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{age }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{bmi }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{smoke }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dibpat, }
             \DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_ms_train)}
\NormalTok{modelB <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(chol) }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{age }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{bmi }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{smoke }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dibpat, }
             \DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_ms_train)}
\NormalTok{modelC <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(chol) }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{age }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{smoke, }
             \DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_ms_train)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\hypertarget{could-we-have-fit-other-models}{%
\subsection{Could we have fit other models?}\label{could-we-have-fit-other-models}}

Sure. One approach would have been to consider an automated variable selection tool, even though they're all pretty terrible. Stepwise regression is a common, if poor, choice for this task. The problem is that there's no good tool for this task, essentially, as we'll discuss further in 432.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  A stepwise regression applied to \texttt{modelA} in the training sample with backwards elimination suggests our \texttt{modelB}.
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stats}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{step}\NormalTok{(modelA)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Start:  AIC=1357.64
sqrt(chol) ~ age + bmi + sbp + dbp + smoke + dibpat

         Df Sum of Sq    RSS    AIC
- sbp     1     0.359 3916.0 1355.8
<none>                3915.6 1357.6
- bmi     1     5.044 3920.6 1358.2
- dibpat  1    10.321 3925.9 1360.9
- age     1    22.217 3937.8 1367.0
- dbp     1    28.014 3943.6 1369.9
- smoke   1    46.322 3961.9 1379.2

Step:  AIC=1355.82
sqrt(chol) ~ age + bmi + dbp + smoke + dibpat

         Df Sum of Sq    RSS    AIC
<none>                3916.0 1355.8
- bmi     1     4.858 3920.8 1356.3
- dibpat  1    10.204 3926.2 1359.0
- age     1    21.913 3937.9 1365.0
- smoke   1    46.021 3962.0 1377.2
- dbp     1    52.228 3968.2 1380.3
\end{verbatim}

\begin{verbatim}

Call:
lm(formula = sqrt(chol) ~ age + bmi + dbp + smoke + dibpat, data = wcgs_ms_train)

Coefficients:
 (Intercept)           age           bmi           dbp      smokeYes  
    12.03138       0.01930       0.02074       0.01835       0.30687  
dibpatType B  
    -0.14393  
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  A stepwise regression with forward selection from the intercept only model moving towards \texttt{modelA} in the training sample also suggests \texttt{modelB}.
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{min_model <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(chol) }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_ms_train)}
\NormalTok{biggest <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{formula}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{lm}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(chol) }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{age }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{bmi }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{sbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dbp }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{smoke }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{dibpat, }
             \DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_ms_train))}
\NormalTok{fwd_step <-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{stats}\OperatorTok{::}\KeywordTok{step}\NormalTok{(min_model, }\DataTypeTok{direction =} \StringTok{'forward'}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{scope =}\NormalTok{ biggest)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Start:  AIC=1430.36
sqrt(chol) ~ 1

         Df Sum of Sq    RSS    AIC
+ dbp     1    83.336 4001.7 1391.1
+ sbp     1    57.718 4027.3 1403.9
+ smoke   1    39.557 4045.5 1412.9
+ age     1    39.060 4046.0 1413.1
+ dibpat  1    20.740 4064.3 1422.2
+ bmi     1    19.788 4065.2 1422.7
<none>                4085.0 1430.4

Step:  AIC=1391.13
sqrt(chol) ~ dbp

         Df Sum of Sq    RSS    AIC
+ smoke   1    45.953 3955.7 1370.0
+ age     1    24.171 3977.5 1381.0
+ dibpat  1    16.675 3985.0 1384.8
<none>                4001.7 1391.1
+ bmi     1     1.964 3999.7 1392.2
+ sbp     1     0.766 4000.9 1392.8

Step:  AIC=1370.04
sqrt(chol) ~ dbp + smoke

         Df Sum of Sq    RSS    AIC
+ age     1   24.3512 3931.4 1359.7
+ dibpat  1   13.4535 3942.3 1365.2
+ bmi     1    4.7652 3951.0 1369.6
<none>                3955.7 1370.0
+ sbp     1    0.0080 3955.7 1372.0

Step:  AIC=1359.69
sqrt(chol) ~ dbp + smoke + age

         Df Sum of Sq    RSS    AIC
+ dibpat  1   10.5635 3920.8 1356.3
+ bmi     1    5.2180 3926.2 1359.0
<none>                3931.4 1359.7
+ sbp     1    0.0906 3931.3 1361.6

Step:  AIC=1356.3
sqrt(chol) ~ dbp + smoke + age + dibpat

       Df Sum of Sq    RSS    AIC
+ bmi   1    4.8581 3916.0 1355.8
<none>              3920.8 1356.3
+ sbp   1    0.1735 3920.6 1358.2

Step:  AIC=1355.82
sqrt(chol) ~ dbp + smoke + age + dibpat + bmi

       Df Sum of Sq    RSS    AIC
<none>              3916.0 1355.8
+ sbp   1   0.35901 3915.6 1357.6
\end{verbatim}

It is tempting to conclude that \texttt{modelB} is somehow a good model, since both forwards and stepwise regression like \texttt{modelB} in our training sample given the predictors we fed to the algorithm. Resist that temptation. As you'll see, even with the pretty simple validation strategy we're going to use here, we won't wind up selecting model B.

\hypertarget{coefficients-of-our-3-models-with-tidy}{%
\subsection{\texorpdfstring{Coefficients of our 3 models with \texttt{tidy}}{Coefficients of our 3 models with tidy}}\label{coefficients-of-our-3-models-with-tidy}}

Given the large sample size, let's look at some 99\% confidence intervals for our model coefficients. We'll use \texttt{tidy} from the \texttt{broom} package.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{tidy}\NormalTok{(modelA, }\DataTypeTok{conf.int =} \OtherTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{conf.level =} \FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{digits =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tabular}{l|r|r|r|r}
\hline
term & estimate & conf.low & conf.high & p.value\\
\hline
(Intercept) & 12.058 & 10.933 & 13.183 & 0.000\\
\hline
age & 0.019 & 0.005 & 0.034 & 0.001\\
\hline
bmi & 0.021 & -0.013 & 0.055 & 0.109\\
\hline
sbp & -0.001 & -0.010 & 0.007 & 0.669\\
\hline
dbp & 0.020 & 0.006 & 0.034 & 0.000\\
\hline
smokeYes & 0.310 & 0.146 & 0.475 & 0.000\\
\hline
dibpatType B & -0.145 & -0.308 & 0.018 & 0.022\\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{tidy}\NormalTok{(modelB, }\DataTypeTok{conf.int =} \OtherTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{conf.level =} \FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{digits =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tabular}{l|r|r|r|r}
\hline
term & estimate & conf.low & conf.high & p.value\\
\hline
(Intercept) & 12.031 & 10.918 & 13.145 & 0.000\\
\hline
age & 0.019 & 0.004 & 0.034 & 0.001\\
\hline
bmi & 0.021 & -0.013 & 0.055 & 0.116\\
\hline
dbp & 0.018 & 0.009 & 0.028 & 0.000\\
\hline
smokeYes & 0.307 & 0.143 & 0.470 & 0.000\\
\hline
dibpatType B & -0.144 & -0.307 & 0.019 & 0.023\\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{tidy}\NormalTok{(modelC, }\DataTypeTok{conf.int =} \OtherTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{conf.level =} \FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{digits =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tabular}{l|r|r|r|r}
\hline
term & estimate & conf.low & conf.high & p.value\\
\hline
(Intercept) & 12.243 & 11.325 & 13.160 & 0\\
\hline
age & 0.020 & 0.005 & 0.035 & 0\\
\hline
