\hypertarget{anova-comparison-of-the-3-models}{%
\subsection{ANOVA comparison of the 3 models}\label{anova-comparison-of-the-3-models}}

Since model C is a subset of model B which is a subset of model A, we can compare these models with ANOVA tests.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{anova}\NormalTok{(modelC, modelB, modelA)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Analysis of Variance Table

Model 1: sqrt(chol) ~ age + dbp + smoke
Model 2: sqrt(chol) ~ age + bmi + dbp + smoke + dibpat
Model 3: sqrt(chol) ~ age + bmi + sbp + dbp + smoke + dibpat
  Res.Df    RSS Df Sum of Sq      F Pr(>F)  
1   1996 3931.4                             
2   1994 3916.0  2    15.422 3.9247 0.0199 *
3   1993 3915.6  1     0.359 0.1827 0.6691  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  There appears to be a detectable improvement (using \(\alpha = 0.05\)) for model B as compared to model C, but not if we use \(\alpha = 0.01\).
\item
  The improvement from model B to model A doesn't appear to meet the standard for a statistically detectable impact based on this ANOVA comparison.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{assessing-fit-quality-of-our-3-models-with-glance}{%
\subsection{\texorpdfstring{Assessing Fit Quality of our 3 models with \texttt{glance}}{Assessing Fit Quality of our 3 models with glance}}\label{assessing-fit-quality-of-our-3-models-with-glance}}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{repA <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{glance}\NormalTok{(modelA) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{name =} \StringTok{"modelA"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{repB <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{glance}\NormalTok{(modelB) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{name =} \StringTok{"modelB"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{repC <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{glance}\NormalTok{(modelC) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{name =} \StringTok{"modelC"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{fit_report <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{bind_rows}\NormalTok{(repA, repB, repC)}

\NormalTok{fit_report }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(name, }\DataTypeTok{r2 =}\NormalTok{ r.squared, }\DataTypeTok{adjr2 =}\NormalTok{ adj.r.squared, sigma, }
\NormalTok{           AIC, BIC, nobs, df, }\DataTypeTok{df.res =}\NormalTok{ df.residual) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{digits =} \KeywordTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tabular}{l|r|r|r|r|r|r|r|r}
\hline
name & r2 & adjr2 & sigma & AIC & BIC & nobs & df & df.res\\
\hline
modelA & 0.0415 & 0.0386 & 1.402 & 7035 & 7080 & 2000 & 6 & 1993\\
\hline
modelB & 0.0414 & 0.0390 & 1.401 & 7034 & 7073 & 2000 & 5 & 1994\\
\hline
modelC & 0.0376 & 0.0362 & 1.403 & 7037 & 7065 & 2000 & 3 & 1996\\
\hline
\end{tabular}

Our conclusions are:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Model A has the strongest \(R^2\) value, as it must because it contains the predictors in the other models, and \(R^2\) is greedy.
\item
  Model B has a slightly stronger adjusted \(R^2\) and \(\sigma\) than the other models, and also has the best performance according to AIC.
\item
  Model C (the smallest of these models) shows the best in-sample BIC result.
\item
  None of the differences we observe between these models are particularly large, and none of the models are especially effective, as judged by \(R^2\).
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{develop-residual-plots}{%
\section{Develop Residual Plots}\label{develop-residual-plots}}

We'll use \texttt{augment} from the \texttt{broom} package to calculate our summaries of the quality of fit at the level of the individual observations, and the fact that our fitted values refer to the square root of cholesterol level is fine for assessing assumptions in-sample.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{## requires library(broom) or library(tidymodels)}

\NormalTok{aug_A <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{augment}\NormalTok{(modelA, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_ms_train)}
\NormalTok{aug_B <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{augment}\NormalTok{(modelB, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_ms_train)}
\NormalTok{aug_C <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{augment}\NormalTok{(modelC, }\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_ms_train)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\hypertarget{first-set-of-residual-diagnostics-3-models}{%
\subsection{First Set of Residual Diagnostics (3 models)}\label{first-set-of-residual-diagnostics-3-models}}

For each model, we'll start with the pair of plots that show us:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  the residuals vs.~the fitted values, to check for non-linearity and non-constant variance, as well as\\
\item
  Normal Q-Q plots of the standardized residuals to check for important non-Normality.
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{## requires library(ggrepel)}

\NormalTok{p1a <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_A, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .fitted, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ .resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_A }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{                   }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.resid), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{),}
               \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_A }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{                        }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.resid), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{),}
                    \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id), }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_abline}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{lty =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Model A"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Fitted sqrt(cholesterol)"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Residual"}\NormalTok{) }

\NormalTok{p1b <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_B, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .fitted, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ .resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_B }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{                   }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.resid), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{),}
               \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_B }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{                        }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.resid), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{),}
                    \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id), }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_abline}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{lty =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Model B"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Fitted sqrt(cholesterol)"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Residual"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p1c <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_C, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .fitted, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ .resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_C }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{                   }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.resid), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{),}
               \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_C }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{                        }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.resid), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{),}
                    \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id), }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_abline}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{lty =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Model C"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{caption =} \StringTok{"In each plot, 5 largest |residuals| highlighted in red."}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Fitted sqrt(cholesterol)"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Residual"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p2a <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_A, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{sample =}\NormalTok{ .std.resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_qq}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_qq_line}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Model A: Normal Q-Q Plot"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Model A Standardized Residual"}\NormalTok{, }
         \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Standard Normal Quantiles"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p2b <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_B, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{sample =}\NormalTok{ .std.resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_qq}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_qq_line}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Model B: Normal Q-Q Plot"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Model B Standardized Residual"}\NormalTok{, }
         \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Standard Normal Quantiles"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p2c <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_C, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{sample =}\NormalTok{ .std.resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_qq}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{geom_qq_line}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Model C: Normal Q-Q Plot"}\NormalTok{,}
         \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Model C Standardized Residual"}\NormalTok{, }
         \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Standard Normal Quantiles"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{(p1a }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p2a) }\OperatorTok{/}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{(p1b }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p2b) }\OperatorTok{/}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{(p1c }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p2c) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{plot_annotation}\NormalTok{(}
        \DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Residual Diagnostics: Set 1 for Models A, B, C"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-373-1.pdf}

In each of these plots, I see no clear signs of substantial non-linearity or any substantial problems with the assumptions of constant variance or Normality. No standardized residuals are especially unusual, and there's no sign of a substantial curve or fan shape in the plots of residuals vs.~fitted values.

\hypertarget{second-set-of-residual-diagnostics-3-models}{%
\subsection{Second Set of Residual Diagnostics (3 models)}\label{second-set-of-residual-diagnostics-3-models}}

As a second check on the assumption of non-constant variance, we'll draw scale-location plots. As a second check on the potential for poorly fit or highly leveraged points to influence our results, we'll also run the residuals vs.~leverage plots.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{## requires library(ggrepel)}

\NormalTok{p3a <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_A, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .fitted, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.std.resid)))) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Model A Scale-Location Plot"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Fitted sqrt(cholesterol)"}\NormalTok{, }
       \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Square Root of |Standardized Residual|"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p3b <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_B, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .fitted, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.std.resid)))) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Model B Scale-Location Plot"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Fitted sqrt(cholesterol)"}\NormalTok{, }
       \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Square Root of |Standardized Residual|"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p3c <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_C, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .fitted, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.std.resid)))) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Model C Scale-Location Plot"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Fitted sqrt(cholesterol)"}\NormalTok{, }
       \DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Square Root of |Standardized Residual|"}\NormalTok{)}


\NormalTok{p4a <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_A, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .hat, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ .std.resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_A }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(.cooksd }\OperatorTok{>=}\StringTok{ }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{),}
             \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_A }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(.cooksd }\OperatorTok{>=}\StringTok{ }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{),}
               \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id), }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_vline}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{xintercept =} \DecValTok{3}\OperatorTok{*}\KeywordTok{mean}\NormalTok{(.hat)), }\DataTypeTok{lty =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Model A Residuals vs. Leverage"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{caption =} \StringTok{"Red points indicate Cook's d at least 0.5"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Leverage"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Standardized Residual"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p4b <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_B, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .hat, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ .std.resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_B }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(.cooksd }\OperatorTok{>=}\StringTok{ }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{),}
             \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_B }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(.cooksd }\OperatorTok{>=}\StringTok{ }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{),}
               \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id), }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_vline}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{xintercept =} \DecValTok{3}\OperatorTok{*}\KeywordTok{mean}\NormalTok{(.hat)), }\DataTypeTok{lty =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Model B Residuals vs. Leverage"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{caption =} \StringTok{"Red points indicate Cook's d at least 0.5"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Leverage"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Standardized Residual"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p4c <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(aug_C, }\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ .hat, }\DataTypeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ .std.resid)) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{() }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_point}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_C }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(.cooksd }\OperatorTok{>=}\StringTok{ }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{),}
             \DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_text_repel}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ aug_C }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{filter}\NormalTok{(.cooksd }\OperatorTok{>=}\StringTok{ }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{),}
               \KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ id), }\DataTypeTok{col =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{method =} \StringTok{"loess"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\OperatorTok{~}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{x, }\DataTypeTok{span =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{se =}\NormalTok{ F) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{geom_vline}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{xintercept =} \DecValTok{3}\OperatorTok{*}\KeywordTok{mean}\NormalTok{(.hat)), }\DataTypeTok{lty =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{  }\KeywordTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Model C Residuals vs. Leverage"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{caption =} \StringTok{"Red points indicate Cook's d at least 0.5"}\NormalTok{,}
       \DataTypeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Leverage"}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Standardized Residual"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{(p3a }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p4a) }\OperatorTok{/}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{(p3b }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p4b) }\OperatorTok{/}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{(p3c }\OperatorTok{+}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{p4c) }\OperatorTok{+}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{plot_annotation}\NormalTok{(}
        \DataTypeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Residual Diagnostics: Set 2 for Models A, B, C"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\includegraphics{431-notes_files/figure-latex/unnamed-chunk-374-1.pdf}

Again, I don't see any signs of powerful trends in the scale-location plot for any of the three models, so there's no clear sign of non-constant variance.

For each of the three models, the residuals vs.~leverage plot shows no points highlighted in red (which would indicate substantial influence - a Cook's distance value above 5.) So I don't see anything to worry about.

\hypertarget{numerical-summaries-of-these-measures-all-3-models}{%
\subsection{Numerical Summaries of these measures (all 3 models)}\label{numerical-summaries-of-these-measures-all-3-models}}

We could summarize the largest standardized residual and the largest Cook's distance, for instance, for each model, with something like this.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{aug_A }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(.std.resid), }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id, sqrtchol, .fitted, .resid, .std.resid)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 1 x 5
     id sqrtchol .fitted .resid .std.resid
  <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl>      <dbl>
1 10340     19.7    14.7   5.08       3.63
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{aug_A }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{slice_max}\NormalTok{(.cooksd, }\DataTypeTok{n =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(id, sqrtchol, .fitted, .resid, .hat, .cooksd)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 1 x 6
     id sqrtchol .fitted .resid    .hat .cooksd
  <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>
1 10072     10.6    15.5  -4.90 0.00938  0.0167
\end{verbatim}

\hypertarget{test-sample-comparisons-for-our-3-models}{%
\section{Test Sample Comparisons for our 3 Models}\label{test-sample-comparisons-for-our-3-models}}

Finally, we'll use our three candidate models (A, B and C) to predict the results in our holdout sample of 1132 observations not used to fit these models to see which model performs better in these new data.

Once again, we'll be using \texttt{augment} to do this work. To start, this is straightforward - we just need to specify the new data we want to predict with \texttt{newdata}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{test_A <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{augment}\NormalTok{(modelA, }\DataTypeTok{newdata =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_ms_test) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{mod_n =} \StringTok{"Model A"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{test_B <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{augment}\NormalTok{(modelB, }\DataTypeTok{newdata =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_ms_test) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{mod_n =} \StringTok{"Model B"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{test_C <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{augment}\NormalTok{(modelC, }\DataTypeTok{newdata =}\NormalTok{ wcgs_ms_test) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{mod_n =} \StringTok{"Model C"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Let's look at the first two results using Model A.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{test_A }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{sqrtchol =} \KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(chol)) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(chol, sqrtchol, .fitted, .resid) }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{head}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 2 x 4
   chol sqrtchol .fitted .resid
  <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl>
1   194     13.9    15.2 -1.28 
2   258     16.1    15.5  0.592
\end{verbatim}

As we can see, the \texttt{.fitted} value is trying to predict the square root of \texttt{chol} and not \texttt{chol}. When we are doing validation in our test sample, it is more helpful to get back to the scale of our original outcome. So here, we'd square the \texttt{.fitted} value (to back out of our square root transformation) and get the actual prediction our model makes for \texttt{chol}. Then we can take the observed \texttt{chol} value and subtract the prediction of \texttt{chol} to get a new residual on the scale of our original \texttt{chol} values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{test_res <-}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{bind_rows}\NormalTok{(test_A, test_B, test_C) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{fit_chol =}\NormalTok{ .fitted}\OperatorTok{^}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DataTypeTok{res_chol =}\NormalTok{ chol }\OperatorTok{-}\StringTok{ }\NormalTok{fit_chol) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{select}\NormalTok{(mod_n, id, chol, fit_chol, res_chol, }\KeywordTok{everything}\NormalTok{()) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{arrange}\NormalTok{(id, mod_n)}

\NormalTok{test_res }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }\KeywordTok{head}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 6 x 13
  mod_n    id  chol fit_chol res_chol   age   bmi   sbp   dbp smoke dibpat
  <chr> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr> 
1 Mode~  2003   181     214.    -33.2    42  23.6   110    78 No    Type B
2 Mode~  2003   181     214.    -32.7    42  23.6   110    78 No    Type B
3 Mode~  2003   181     216.    -35.0    42  23.6   110    78 No    Type B
4 Mode~  2004   132     222.    -89.9    41  23.1   124    78 Yes   Type B
5 Mode~  2004   132     222.    -89.9    41  23.1   124    78 Yes   Type B
6 Mode~  2004   132     224.    -92.5    41  23.1   124    78 Yes   Type B
# ... with 2 more variables: .fitted <dbl>, .resid <dbl>
\end{verbatim}

Now, we can summarize the quality of the predictions across each of the models with four summary statistics calculated across the 1132 observations in the test sample.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  the mean absolute prediction error, or MAPE
\item
  the \emph{median} absolute prediction error, or medAPE
\item
  the maximum absolute prediction error, or maxAPE
\item
  the square root of the mean \emph{squared} prediction error, or RMSPE
\end{itemize}

No one of these dominates the other, but we might be interested in which model gives us the best (smallest) result for each of these summaries. Let's run them.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{test_res }\OperatorTok{%>%}\StringTok{ }
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{group_by}\NormalTok{(mod_n) }\OperatorTok{%>%}
\StringTok{    }\KeywordTok{summarise}\NormalTok{(}\DataTypeTok{MAPE =} \KeywordTok{mean}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(res_chol)),}
              \DataTypeTok{medAPE =} \KeywordTok{median}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(res_chol)),}
              \DataTypeTok{maxAPE =} \KeywordTok{max}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{abs}\NormalTok{(res_chol)),}
              \DataTypeTok{RMSPE =} \KeywordTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\KeywordTok{mean}\NormalTok{(res_chol}\OperatorTok{^}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 3 x 5
  mod_n    MAPE medAPE maxAPE RMSPE
  <chr>   <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>
1 Model A  32.2   27.1   151.  40.8
2 Model B  32.2   27.1   150.  40.8
3 Model C  32.1   26.5   147.  40.8
\end{verbatim}

Which model has the best performance in the testing sample?

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Model C has the smallest (best) MAPE, median APR, maximum APE and RMSPE.
\item
  Theres not an enormous difference between the models on most of these summaries, but Model C (the smallest model) does appear to do a bit better.
\end{itemize}

\hypertarget{putting-it-together---which-model-do-we-like-best}{%
\section{Putting it Together - which model do we like best?}\label{putting-it-together---which-model-do-we-like-best}}

It's worth remembering that none of the three models was particularly strong. Even Model A (with the full set of predictors) had an \(R^2\) of only about 4.15\%.

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  All 3 models had reasonable residual plots, so we don't have much to choose from there.
\item
  Within the development sample, Model B had slightly better adjusted \(R^2\), \(\sigma\) and AIC results than the other models, while Model C had the best BIC.
\item
  Model C had the best results in the test sample, across all four summaries we examined.
\end{enumerate}

In this case, I'd pick Model C from among these options, based mainly on the stronger test sample results and the fact that it's a smaller model (with fewer predictors) and if none of the models are going to do very well in terms of predicting \texttt{chol}, there's not much to choose from anyway.

\hypertarget{species-found-on-the-galapagos-islands}{%
\chapter{Species Found on the Galapagos Islands}\label{species-found-on-the-galapagos-islands}}

This chapter was written in 2016 and 2017, before (for example) I knew anything about the \texttt{broom} package. Some of the methods involved are no longer part of what we usually do, but I thought it might be helpful to see some alternate approaches. Feel free to stick with the methods shown in the previous chapters if you want to do something more tidy.

\hypertarget{a-little-background}{%
\section{A Little Background}\label{a-little-background}}

The \texttt{gala} data describe describe features of the 30 Galapagos Islands.

The Galapagos Islands are found about 900 km west of South America: specifically the continental part of Ecuador. The Islands form a province of Ecuador and serve as a national park and marine reserve. They are noted for their vast numbers of unique (or endemic) species and were studied by Charles Darwin during the voyage of the Beagle. I didn't know most of this, but it's on Wikipedia, so I'll assume it's all true until someone sets me straight.

\begin{figure}
\centering
\includegraphics{figures/galapic.jpg}
\caption{galapic.jpg}
\end{figure}

\hypertarget{sources}{%
\subsection{Sources}\label{sources}}

The data were initially presented by Johnson M and Raven P (1973) Species number and endemism: the Galapagos Archipelago revisited. \emph{Science} 179: 893-895 and also appear in several regression texts, including my source: \citet{Faraway2015}. Note that Faraway filled in some missing data to simplify things a bit. A similar version of the data is available as part of the \texttt{faraway} library in R, but I encourage you to use the version I supply on our web site.

\hypertarget{variables-in-the-gala-data-frame}{%
\subsection{\texorpdfstring{Variables in the \texttt{gala} data frame}{Variables in the gala data frame}}\label{variables-in-the-gala-data-frame}}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{id} = island identification code
\item
  \textbf{island} = island name
\item
  \textbf{species} = our outcome, the number of species found on the island
\item
  \textbf{area} = the area of the island, in square kilometers
\item
  \textbf{elevation} = the highest elevation of the island, in meters
\item
  \textbf{nearest} = the distance from the nearest island, in kilometers
\item
  \textbf{scruz} = the distance from Santa Cruz Island, in kilometers. Santa Cruz is the home to the largest human population in the Islands, and to the town of Puerto Ayora.
\item
  \textbf{adjacent} = the area of the adjacent island, in square kilometers
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{gala}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}
